groundIt.clusterTitle: CVD Overview — Cardiac Anatomy, Physiology & Risk Factors
groundIt.content:
Cardiovascular disease covers diseases of the heart and blood vessels. Blood flows: left ventricle → aortic valve → aorta → arteries → arterioles → capillaries → venules → veins → inferior/superior vena cava → right atrium → tricuspid valve → right ventricle → pulmonary valve → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary veins → left atrium → mitral (bicuspid) valve → left ventricle. Arteries are thick-walled, high-pressure, no valves. Veins are thin-walled, low-pressure, have valves. Capillaries are one cell thick (~5 microns).

The four heart valves: aortic (left ventricle to aorta), mitral/bicuspid (left atrium to left ventricle), pulmonary (right ventricle to pulmonary artery), tricuspid (right atrium to right ventricle). The coronary arteries supply the heart itself — the left coronary artery divides into the left anterior descending (LAD — supplies front of heart) and circumflex (back); the right coronary artery supplies the inferior heart.

The cardiac conducting system: sinoatrial (SA) node (right atrium, sets heart rate) → atria contract → atrioventricular (AV) node (detects atrial depolarisation) → Bundle of His → right and left bundle branches → Purkinje fibres → ventricular contraction. ECG: P wave (atrial depolarisation/contraction), PR interval (AV node delay), QRS complex (ventricular depolarisation/contraction), T wave (ventricular repolarisation). The left ventricle is the largest and most muscular chamber.

Modifiable risk factors for CVD: hypertension (most important correctable risk factor — risk doubles for every 20/10 mmHg increase), hyperlipidaemia (raised LDL, low HDL), diabetes mellitus, smoking (carbon monoxide damages endothelial cells), obesity, physical inactivity. Non-modifiable: age (>55), male sex, post-menopausal women, genetics (first-degree relative with CVD under 55), South Asian/African-Caribbean ethnicity.
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Name the four cardiac valves and their positions, then identify the three most important modifiable cardiovascular risk factors and explain why each contributes to CVD.

groundIt.content:
Atherosclerosis is the process of narrowing and hardening of arteries due to atheroma — focal accumulation of lipid in the intima of large and medium-sized arteries. It preferentially occurs at arterial bifurcations (areas of turbulent blood flow) and in the coronary, carotid, cerebral, and femoral arteries.

Normal artery structure: three layers — intima (inner, single endothelial cell layer), tunica media (muscular middle layer), adventitia (outer connective tissue). Atherosclerosis develops in four stages: (1) Endothelial dysfunction — hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, and smoking damage endothelium at areas of turbulent flow. (2) Fatty streak — LDLs and monocytes (which differentiate into macrophages under lymphocyte cytokine influence) enter the intima. Macrophages engulf LDL but cannot digest the fat → 'foamy macrophages' (the characteristic histological feature of the early lesion). (3) Fibrolipid plaque — smooth muscle cells migrate from media into intima; produce collagen; a fibrous cap forms over a lipid-rich core; luminal bulging begins. Elevated lesion, endothelial lining still intact. (4) Complicated lesion — endothelial cells stripped away; platelets impact on raw surface → clotting cascade → thrombus formation; calcification; haemorrhage into plaque; plaque fissure; embolism.

Consequences of atherosclerosis: ischaemic heart disease (angina, MI, heart failure) in coronary arteries; cerebral ischaemia and infarction (TIA, stroke) in carotid/cerebral vessels; peripheral vascular disease (intermittent claudication — pain in calves on walking) in femoral arteries; aortic aneurysm; mesenteric ischaemia.

Virchow's triad (for thrombosis): endothelial damage + abnormal blood flow + hypercoagulability. A thrombus is a solid clot in flowing blood (contains platelets, fibrin, and red cells — lines of Zahn visible histologically). An embolus is material travelling through the circulation. Most emboli are thromboemboli. Others: fat, air, bone marrow, amniotic fluid, tumour, foreign material. Pulmonary embolism: DVT travels via inferior vena cava to pulmonary artery. Paradoxical embolus: crosses patent foramen ovale to become systemic. Risk factors for DVT: immobility, surgery, pregnancy, malignancy, age, congestive heart failure, burns.
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Describe the four stages of atherosclerotic plaque development, naming the characteristic histological feature of the fatty streak stage, and state three consequences of a complicated lesion.

groundIt.clusterTitle: Ischaemic Heart Disease, Angina & Myocardial Infarction
groundIt.content:
Ischaemic heart disease (IHD) encompasses the spectrum of disorders caused by coronary artery atherosclerosis and thrombosis. It includes angina (reversible ischaemia — chest pain on exertion from narrowed but not completely blocked coronary arteries), myocardial infarction (irreversible damage from complete coronary artery occlusion), and heart failure secondary to ischaemic damage.

Angina SOCRATES: Site — central chest; Onset — on exertion (stable angina) or at rest (unstable angina/ACS); Characteristics — tight, squeezing, band-like (NOT sharp); Radiation — neck, left arm, jaw (mandible); Associated — breathlessness, sweatiness, nausea; Time — <5 minutes in stable angina (hours in ACS); Exacerbating/relieving — worse on exertion, improved by rest or GTN spray. Diagnosis: ECG, exercise ECG (stress test), CT coronary angiography, myocardial perfusion scan, cardiac MRI.

MI: caused by plaque rupture at a vulnerable plaque → coronary thrombosis → complete occlusion. Evolution: <6 hours — no visible changes but ECG changes (ST elevation or depression); 24–48 hours — pallor with haemorrhagic rim; several days — pale, grey, fibrotic. Haemopericardium (blood in pericardial sac) greatest risk at 4–6 hours post-MI — can cause tamponade and sudden death. Dental management in post-MI patients: postpone elective treatment for 3–6 months. GTN spray used for angina attacks in chair: 2 puffs sublingually, repeat every 3 minutes — if needs >2 doses, suspect MI, call ambulance. In MI: 100% O2 at 15L/min, sublingual GTN, aspirin 300mg chewed.

Drug management of angina: nitrates (GTN — NO donor → smooth muscle relaxation → venodilation reduces cardiac preload → coronary artery dilation; administered sublingual, oral, transdermal, IV — nitrate tolerance requires 'nitrate-free period' usually overnight); beta-blockers (decrease heart rate and contractility, reduce myocardial oxygen demand); calcium channel blockers (dihydropyridines like amlodipine — vasodilation; benzothiazines like diltiazem — slow HR slightly; phenylalkylamines like verapamil — dramatically slow HR; NEVER combine verapamil with beta-blockers — cardiac arrest risk); potassium channel openers (nicorandil — vasodilation + nitrate component; causes oral ulceration); ivabradine (selective If channel blocker — lowers heart rate only); ranolazine (inhibitor of late sodium current).
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Describe the character and radiation of anginal chest pain and explain how you would distinguish it from an MI in the dental chair, including your immediate management steps for each.

groundIt.content:
Valvular heart disease: two main types — stenosis (valve narrowed, cannot open fully → obstruction to blood flow; most common: aortic stenosis) and regurgitation (valve leaky, cannot close fully → backflow; most common in UK: mitral regurgitation). Patients present with dizziness, syncope, breathlessness, and less commonly chest tightness. Diagnosis: echocardiography (transthoracic, or transesophageal for more detail). Treatment options: medical management, keyhole (TAVI — transcatheter aortic valve implantation for stenosis; transcatheter edge-to-edge repair for regurgitation), or surgical valve replacement (tissue or mechanical). Mechanical valves require lifelong anticoagulation. Risk: infective endocarditis.

Infective endocarditis (IE): bacterial (most commonly), rarely viral or fungal. Predominantly affects the left heart (mitral and aortic valves, 95% of cases). Pathogenesis: bacteraemia deposits on damaged/irregular valve surface → vegetation formation (bacteria + platelets + fibrin) → further bacterial deposition. Organisms: oral streptococci (most common cause in patients with pre-existing valve disease), Staphylococcus aureus (acute IE — equally prevalent on normal and abnormal valves; 25–47% mortality), HACEK organisms (Haemophilus, Aggregatibacter, Cardiobacterium, Eikenella, Kingella — 5% of cases), Candida/Aspergillus (~1%).

Risk factors: structural — congenital lesions (VSD, tetralogy of Fallot, patent ductus arteriosus), valvular abnormalities (rheumatic fever, bicuspid aortic valve, mitral valve prolapse), prosthetic valves. Non-structural: IV drug abuse, surgical/dental procedures causing bacteraemia. Clinical features: pyrexia (most common), sweats, malaise, weight loss, anaemia, splenomegaly. Skin signs: Osler's nodes (painful raised lumps on fingers/toes), Janeway lesions, splinter haemorrhages (>8 indicate endocarditis), petechiae. Eyes: Roth spots (retinal haemorrhages), subconjunctival haemorrhages. Septic emboli can travel to brain, limbs, kidneys (glomerulonephritis → haematuria). Diagnosis: echocardiogram, blood cultures (may be negative in 14% of cases). Duke criteria: 2 major criteria, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor → definite IE.

NICE guidelines on antibiotic prophylaxis: not routinely recommended for dental procedures. SDCEP stratifies risk — high risk: previous IE, prosthetic valves/material, cyanotic congenital heart disease, repaired CHD with prosthetic material (first 6 months or lifelong if residual shunt/regurgitation). Moderate risk: previous rheumatic fever with valve damage, native valve disease, unrepaired congenital valve anomalies. Arguments against prophylaxis: no consistent link between dental procedures and IE; daily tooth-brushing presents greater cumulative bacteraemia risk than a single procedure; prophylaxis is not cost-effective and risk of anaphylaxis may exceed IE risk. Key patient advice: importance of oral hygiene (most important prevention!), seek medical attention if IE symptoms develop, avoid body piercing/tattooing.
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Explain the pathogenesis of infective endocarditis following a dental extraction, and describe two skin signs that might lead a clinician to suspect it.

groundIt.content:
Heart failure is a clinical syndrome (breathlessness, fatigue, oedema) caused by failure of the heart to pump adequately. Classified as: acute or chronic; right-sided or left-sided. Left-sided failure: blood dams back in pulmonary veins → raised pulmonary venous pressure → pulmonary oedema → dyspnoea on exertion progressing to orthopnoea (breathlessness lying flat — important for dental chair positioning), paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, pink frothy sputum. Right-sided failure (Cor pulmonale): blood dams in systemic veins → peripheral oedema (ankles, sacrum), raised JVP, hepatic congestion, ascites, fatigue.

Common cardiac causes: ischaemic heart disease (MI → left ventricular damage → systolic dysfunction), hypertension (increased afterload → LV hypertrophy), valvular disease (stenosis or regurgitation), sustained arrhythmias (especially AF). Non-cardiac causes: anaemia (high output), hyperthyroidism, AV shunts, IV fluid overload, renal failure.

Neurohormonal mechanisms in heart failure are paradoxical and ultimately harmful despite initially compensatory intent: (1) Reduced CO/BP resembles dehydration → RAAS activation (aldosterone → retain Na and water → oedema; vasoconstriction) + sympathetic activation (adrenaline/noradrenaline → tachycardia and vasoconstriction → maintain BP); (2) Raised intracardiac pressure → ANP and BNP (brain natriuretic peptide) released → promote sodium excretion. Long-term sympathetic activation: fluid retention, vasoconstriction increases resistance (afterload), continuous sympathetic stimulation damages myocardium.

Drug treatment of heart failure: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril — inhibit RAAS; dilate blood vessels; reduce preload and afterload; improve survival); ARBs (losartan — if ACE inhibitor cough intolerable; don't cause cough as no bradykinin effect); beta-blockers (stabilise rhythm; reduce sympathetic damage; for STABLE heart failure only — may precipitate failure in unstable patients); loop diuretics (furosemide — most powerful, increase urine tenfold; correct fluid overload; cause hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, ototoxicity); aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone — potassium-sparing; improves survival; causes gynaecomastia; hyperkalaemia risk); digoxin (cardiac glycoside — inhibits Na/K-ATPase pump → calcium accumulates → positive inotrope; vagolytic → slows AV conduction; for AF + heart failure; narrow therapeutic index; toxicity: visual disturbances, confusion, arrhythmias, nausea). Non-pharmacological: cardiac resynchronisation pacemaker (CRT), valve repair, heart transplantation.
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Explain why a patient with left-sided heart failure cannot lie flat for dental treatment, linking your answer to the pathophysiology of orthopnoea.

groundIt.content:
Arrhythmias are disorders of heart rate or rhythm. Classified as tachyarrhythmias (>100 bpm), bradyarrhythmias (<60 bpm), or cardiac arrest. ECG is the key diagnostic tool. Tachyarrhythmias are divided into supraventricular (arise above Bundle of His — narrow QRS complex on ECG, generally not immediately life-threatening) and ventricular (arise in ventricular tissue — broad/slurred QRS, can cause cardiac arrest).

Atrial fibrillation (AF): most common rhythm disturbance. Uncoordinated atrial activity → irregular, rapid ventricular response. Can cause heart failure and stroke (thrombus forms in fibrillating atria → emboli → cerebral infarction → anticoagulation with warfarin or DOAC is key). Managed with: digoxin or beta-blockers (rate control); DC cardioversion or amiodarone (rhythm control); warfarin or DOACs to reduce stroke risk. Ventricular tachycardia (VT): rate >140 bpm; associated with IHD; can cause cardiac arrest. Ventricular fibrillation (VF): completely disorganised — no cardiac output, cardiac arrest, death within minutes. Most common cause of sudden cardiac death post-MI.

Antiarrhythmic drugs (Vaughan Williams classification): Class I — sodium channel blockers (e.g. lidocaine — Class Ib, fast sodium channel block, used for ventricular arrhythmias; short half-life, IV infusion; can cause hypotension, heart block, and neurotoxicity); Class II — beta-blockers (antisympathetic, reduce sinus node rate, used for SVT and AF); Class III — potassium channel blockers (amiodarone — prolongs action potential duration and refractory period; first-line for VF; multiple serious adverse effects: thyroid disturbances, pulmonary fibrosis, pro-arrhythmia, peripheral neuropathy, irreversible blue-grey skin discolouration); Class IV — calcium channel blockers (verapamil — slows AV node conduction, used for SVT; NEVER combine with beta-blockers — cardiac arrest risk; can cause constipation, heart failure); adenosine (used for acute SVT — transiently blocks AV node, terminates re-entry circuits).

Bradyarrhythmias: sinus bradycardia, heart block (1st degree — prolonged PR; 2nd degree — some impulses not conducted; 3rd degree/complete — no impulses conducted, patient needs pacemaker). Drugs to increase heart rate: atropine (antimuscarinic — blocks vagal slowing of AV node; IV; dry mouth, urinary retention, mydriasis as side effects) and isoprenaline (beta-1/2 agonist — increases sinus node rate). Implantable cardiac devices: pacemakers and defibrillators — newer models less susceptible to electromagnetic interference from dental equipment, but older devices may be affected by ultrasonic scalers.
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Explain why atrial fibrillation increases the risk of stroke and what drug class is given to reduce this risk. Then describe the Vaughan Williams classification and give one drug for each of the four classes.

groundIt.content:
Hypertension is elevated blood pressure — systolic >140 mmHg and/or diastolic >90 mmHg. Risk of CVD doubles for every 20/10 mmHg increase in BP. Often asymptomatic — 'the silent killer'. Measured with sphygmomanometer above the brachial artery; requires multiple readings and ideally ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) to avoid white coat effect.

Stages: Stage 1 — ≥140/90 mmHg (clinic) or ≥135/85 ABPM; Stage 2 — ≥160/100 mmHg; Severe — ≥180/110 mmHg. Stage 1: lifestyle modification, ABPM. Stage 2: medication + lifestyle. Severe: immediate treatment, may be emergency.

Causes: 90% essential (idiopathic — polygenic + environmental: salt intake, obesity, inactivity); 10% secondary — most commonly renal (renal artery stenosis in older smokers, glomerulonephritis in younger patients); endocrine (Conn's syndrome — hyperaldosteronism from adrenal adenoma; Cushing's syndrome — excess cortisol; phaeochromocytoma — adrenal catecholamine tumour); coarctation of aorta; drugs (amphetamines, cocaine).

Renin-angiotensin system: kidney senses reduced perfusion → renin release → angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → ACE → angiotensin II → binds AT1 receptor → aldosterone secretion (sodium/water retention) + peripheral vasoconstriction + sympathetic activation → raised BP.

Drug treatment (ABCD): A — ACE inhibitors (lisinopril: inhibit conversion of Ang I to Ang II; adverse: cough, angioedema, hyperkalaemia, renal failure in bilateral renal artery stenosis, fetal injury) or ARBs (losartan: antagonise AT1 receptor; no cough; similar adverse effects except cough); B — Beta-blockers (atenolol: cardioselective; adverse: bronchospasm, cold extremities, blunting of hypoglycaemia awareness, tooth demineralisation, lichenoid reactions, fatigue); C — Calcium channel blockers (amlodipine: dihydropyridine, vasodilates, little cardiac effect; gingival hyperplasia; verapamil/diltiazem also slow HR); D — Diuretics (indapamide: thiazide-like, blocks Na-Cl cotransport, causes hypokalaemia, hyponatraemia, gout). ABCD drug combinations: A+C or A+D preferred; B+D increases new-onset diabetes risk — avoid. Under 55: start with A → add C → add D → add alpha-blocker or extra diuretic. Over 55 or African/Caribbean: start with C → add A → add D.

Stroke: neurological deficit of presumed vascular cause lasting >24 hours. TIA resolves within 24 hours. 85% ischaemic (thrombosis or embolism — AF causing emboli is a major cause); 15% haemorrhagic (rupture of vessel — hypertension causes haemorrhagic strokes). Symptoms depend on which cerebral artery is affected. 100,000 new cases/year in UK; 3rd most common cause of death and severe disability.
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Describe the renin-angiotensin system and explain how ACE inhibitors reduce blood pressure. Then name two adverse effects of ACE inhibitors relevant to dentistry.

groundIt.content:
Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) is narrowing of arteries (mainly leg arteries) due to atherosclerosis. Main symptom: intermittent claudication — pain in calves (occasionally buttocks/thighs) on walking, relieved within minutes by standing still (lactic acid washout). Diagnosed by ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) <0.9 using handheld Doppler; confirmed with CT/MR angiography. PAD has a high mortality: 20% die from MI within 5 years; 3% require amputation. Management: antiplatelet (clopidogrel preferred over aspirin), statins, antihypertensives, ACE inhibitors, control of diabetes, smoking cessation, exercise.

Critical ischaemia: ischaemia threatening limb loss. Rest pain in toes/feet (worse at night; patient hangs foot out of bed to use gravity), ulcers or gangrene. Doppler pressure <50mmHg in foot. If untreated, 70% need amputation. Treatment: revascularisation (balloon angioplasty, stenting, or bypass surgery).

Acute limb ischaemia: sudden onset from thrombosis or embolism. 6 Ps: Pain, Pallor, Pulseless, Paraesthesia, Paralysis, Perishingly cold (poikilothermia). Irreversible damage after 4–6 hours. Emergency: heparin + analgesia immediately; surgical embolectomy or bypass.

Aortic aneurysm: >50% dilation of the aorta (>3cm). Most common site: infrarenal abdominal aorta (AAA). Risk factors: male, >65, smoking, hypertension, atherosclerosis. Repair when >5.5cm (risk of rupture exceeds surgical risk). Treatment: endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR — stent via femoral artery) or open surgical repair. Screened with ultrasound (100% sensitivity). Cerebral aneurysms (berry aneurysms): most common site for saccular aneurysms — rupture causes subarachnoid haemorrhage (sudden severe headache — 'thunderclap headache'). Aortic dissection: intimal tear in aortic wall → blood tracks between layers. Caused by hypertension (most common), connective tissue disorders, trauma.
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Describe the symptoms of intermittent claudication and explain why this symptom is specifically relieved by standing still (not sitting). Then name the investigation used to confirm peripheral arterial disease and state the diagnostic threshold.

groundIt.content:
Haemostasis: four phases — vasoconstriction → platelet plug formation → coagulation cascade → fibrinolysis. Platelets activated by ADP and thromboxane A2 → become adhesive → bind to endothelium via GP1a receptor (direct) or via von Willebrand factor (GP1b receptor) → fibrinogen links adjacent platelets → coagulation cascade forms fibrin mesh. Common pathway: Factor X activated → Factor V → prothrombin (Factor II) → thrombin → fibrinogen (Factor I) → fibrin. Extrinsic pathway: tissue factor + Factor VII → activates common pathway. Intrinsic: Factor XII → XI → IX → VIII → common pathway.

Antiplatelet drugs: (1) Aspirin — irreversible COX-1 inhibitor → blocks thromboxane A2 → antiplatelet for 7–10 days. Used: prophylaxis in IHD, previous MI/stroke, angina. 75mg/day prophylactic; 300mg in acute MI. Risk: post-operative haemorrhage (local measures usually sufficient — do not discontinue). Contraindicated: allergy, age <12, active peptic ulceration, recent GI/intracranial bleeding, severe liver disease. (2) Clopidogrel — ADP receptor antagonist (P2Y12 inhibitor); safer GI profile than aspirin; used in acute coronary syndrome (ACS), often dual antiplatelet with aspirin. (3) Dipyridamole — adenosine reuptake inhibitor; adjunct with warfarin for prosthetic valves; contraindicated in unstable angina.

Anticoagulants: (1) Heparin — enhances antithrombin III activity → inactivates thrombin and Factor Xa; given by injection only; immediate onset; half-life 1–5 hours; monitored by APTT; reversed by protamine sulphate. Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH — dalteparin, enoxaparin): subcutaneous, once daily, no APTT monitoring needed, less thrombocytopenia risk, less reversible than unfractionated heparin. (2) Warfarin — oral vitamin K antagonist; inhibits clotting factors II, VII, IX, X (all vitamin K-dependent); half-life 35–37 hours; extensively protein bound (98%); interacts with 'everything' (metronidazole, fluconazole/miconazole, erythromycin, amoxicillin + warfarin → all raise INR by displacing warfarin from protein binding). Monitored by INR (normal without anticoagulation ~1; therapeutic range 2–4 for most conditions). Dental management on warfarin: proceed if INR ≤4; check INR within 72 hours pre-procedure (ideally on the day); use local haemostatic measures (oxidised cellulose, gelatin sponge, collagen, tranexamic acid mouthwash); never prescribe NSAIDs or COX-2 inhibitors (further impair haemostasis). (3) DOACs (direct oral anticoagulants): dabigatran (direct thrombin inhibitor, Xa inhibitor class); rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban (direct Factor Xa inhibitors). Linear dose response (predictable, no monitoring needed). Renal excretion — reduce dose/avoid in renal failure. Dental management: INR not useful; for low-risk procedures (1–3 simple extractions) proceed without alteration; for higher-risk procedures (complex extractions, flap surgery), skip morning dose if twice daily, ensure ≥4 hours after haemostasis before taking next dose.

Thrombolytics: streptokinase, alteplase (tPA) — plasminogen activators; dissolve existing clots; used in acute coronary thrombosis and acute ischaemic stroke (alteplase within 4.5 hours).
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Explain the INR monitoring system for warfarin and state the threshold at which dental procedures should be deferred. Then name two drug interactions that significantly raise INR and explain the mechanism.

groundIt.clusterTitle: Drugs for CVD & Dental Implications of Cardiovascular Disease
groundIt.content:
Drugs for heart failure specifically: digoxin (cardiac glycoside — inhibits Na/K-ATPase → intracellular Na rises → Ca accumulates → positive inotrope; vagolytic → slows AV conduction; narrow therapeutic index; toxicity: nausea, visual disturbances including yellow-green vision, confusion, arrhythmias; monitoring of plasma digoxin levels required); furosemide (loop diuretic — inhibits Na/K/Cl transporter in loop of Henle; most potent diuretic — 10-fold increase in urine; hypokalaemia and hyponatraemia are major risks — need regular electrolyte monitoring; also ototoxicity, gout, pancreatitis); spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist — potassium-sparing; pro-drug; oestrogenic effects — gynaecomastia, menstrual disturbances; hyperkalaemia risk; improves survival in CHF).

Dental implications of antihypertensive therapy: beta-blockers → xerostomia, lichenoid eruptions, increased tooth demineralisation; diuretics → xerostomia, lichenoid reactions; ACE inhibitors → xerostomia, angioedema (tongue swelling — emergency!), burning mouth syndrome, altered taste (captopril); calcium channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine) → gingival hyperplasia, xerostomia; nicorandil → oral ulceration (can cause severe non-healing oral ulcers); statins → lichenoid reactions.

Dental management of patients with significant CVD: morning appointments (cortisol peaks, cardiac events more common in afternoon); shorter appointments; effective LA (pain and anxiety are physiologically equivalent to stress — both trigger catecholamine release, raising HR and BP and increasing myocardial oxygen demand); vasoconstrictor use — patients with mild-to-moderate CVD can receive adrenaline-containing LA (the physiological adrenaline surge from pain and anxiety exceeds the dose in dental cartridges); for severe CVD (unstable angina, recent MI, severe uncontrolled hypertension, severe CHF), vasoconstrictors are relatively contraindicated. Conscious sedation only in stable patients. Post-op analgesia: avoid NSAIDs in heart failure (sodium retention) and in anticoagulated patients. Monitor: BP, pulse oximetry, ECG in high-risk patients. Medical emergencies in CVD: angina — GTN spray (2 puffs sublingual, repeat every 3 min, max 3 doses); MI — O2 15L/min, GTN spray, aspirin 300mg chewed, call 999; cardiac arrest — CPR, AED.

Oral manifestations of CVD drugs: xerostomia (beta-blockers, diuretics, ACE inhibitors, CCBs); lichenoid eruptions (NSAIDs, beta-blockers, diuretics, oral hypoglycaemics, statins); gingival hyperplasia (amlodipine, nifedipine, cyclosporin, phenytoin); angioedema (ACE inhibitors). Periodontal disease is a risk factor for coronary artery disease — the shared inflammatory pathway (TNF-alpha, IL-6, CRP) linking periodontal bacteria to systemic inflammation and atherosclerosis.
groundIt.selfExplainPrompt: Name four oral adverse effects of cardiovascular drugs, linking each to a specific drug class. Then explain why a patient with left-sided heart failure requires special considerations for their dental appointment.

recallIt.question: Name the four cardiac valves and their positions.
recallIt.answer:
Aortic: between left ventricle and aorta
Mitral/bicuspid: between left atrium and left ventricle
Pulmonary: between right ventricle and pulmonary artery
Tricuspid: between right atrium and right ventricle

recallIt.question: What are the stages of ECG waveform and what does each represent?
recallIt.answer:
P wave: atrial depolarisation (contraction)
PR interval: delay at AV node
QRS complex: ventricular depolarisation (contraction)
ST segment: pause
T wave: ventricular repolarisation

recallIt.question: What is Virchow's triad?
recallIt.answer:
The three conditions predisposing to thrombosis:
1. Endothelial damage
2. Abnormal blood flow (stasis or turbulence)
3. Hypercoagulability

recallIt.question: What is the characteristic histological feature of the fatty streak in atherosclerosis?
recallIt.answer: Foamy macrophages — macrophages that have engulfed LDL cholesterol but cannot digest it, so fill with fatty droplets and sit in the intima of the artery.

recallIt.question: What is the SOCRATES description of typical anginal pain?
recallIt.answer:
Site: central chest
Onset: on exertion (stable) or rest (unstable)
Character: tight, squeezing, band-like — NOT sharp or stabbing
Radiation: left arm, neck, jaw (mandible)
Associated: breathlessness, sweating, nausea
Time: <5 minutes in stable angina (resolves with rest or GTN)
Exacerbating: exercise, stress
Relieving: rest, GTN spray

recallIt.question: What are the NICE and SDCEP guidelines on antibiotic prophylaxis for infective endocarditis?
recallIt.answer:
NICE (2016): antibiotic prophylaxis NOT routinely recommended for dental procedures in at-risk patients.
SDCEP: high risk (previous IE, prosthetic valves, cyanotic CHD) and moderate risk (rheumatic fever with valve damage, native valve disease) are identified — but prophylaxis is still not routinely given.
Most important prevention: excellent oral hygiene to reduce the daily bacteraemia burden from tooth-brushing.

recallIt.question: What are the HACEK organisms associated with infective endocarditis?
recallIt.answer:
H — Haemophilus
A — Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans
C — Cardiobacterium hominis
E — Eikenella corrodens
K — Kingella spp
Account for approximately 5% of IE cases.

recallIt.question: What are the skin signs of infective endocarditis?
recallIt.answer:
Osler's nodes: painful raised lesions on finger/toe pulps
Janeway lesions: painless erythematous macules on palms/soles
Splinter haemorrhages: longitudinal dark lines under nails (>8 suggest endocarditis)
Finger clubbing
Petechiae (skin rash)

recallIt.question: Why should verapamil and beta-blockers never be combined?
recallIt.answer: Both drugs significantly slow the heart: beta-blockers by blocking sympathetic stimulation; verapamil (a phenylalkylamine calcium channel blocker) by slowing calcium-dependent conduction in the SA and AV nodes. Combined, they can completely stop the heart — cardiac arrest. Use either diltiazem or dihydropyridines (amlodipine) with beta-blockers instead.

recallIt.question: What are the dental implications of a patient taking warfarin?
recallIt.answer:
1. Check INR within 72 hours pre-procedure (ideally on the day)
2. Proceed if INR ≤4 — local haemostatic measures sufficient
3. Do NOT stop warfarin — risk of thromboembolism exceeds bleeding risk
4. Do NOT prescribe NSAIDs, COX-2 inhibitors, aspirin, or metronidazole (raise INR)
5. Use oxidised cellulose, gelatin sponge, sutures, and tranexamic acid mouthwash for haemostasis
6. Warn patient about increased bruising/bleeding

recallIt.question: What are the two most important things about digoxin that make it clinically challenging?
recallIt.answer:
1. Narrow therapeutic index — the toxic dose is only slightly higher than the therapeutic dose; plasma levels must be monitored
2. Renal excretion — half-life is markedly prolonged in renal failure (drug accumulates → toxicity). Signs of digoxin toxicity: nausea, visual disturbances (yellow-green tinge), confusion, bradyarrhythmias, supraventricular and ventricular arrhythmias.

recallIt.question: What oral adverse effects are caused by calcium channel blockers?
recallIt.answer:
Gingival hyperplasia (dihydropyridines — amlodipine, nifedipine; exacerbated by poor oral hygiene; management: improve OH, refer to periodontist, consider drug substitution with physician)
Xerostomia
Flushing and oedema (due to vasodilation)

recallIt.question: What is the dental management of a patient who presents with an acute angina attack in the chair?
recallIt.answer:
1. Stop treatment; remain calm
2. Allow patient to sit upright (or comfortable position)
3. Administer their own GTN spray — 2 puffs sublingual
4. Wait 3–5 minutes
5. If no improvement: repeat GTN (2 more puffs)
6. If still no improvement after second dose: suspect MI — call 999; give O2 15L/min, aspirin 300mg chewed
7. Monitor pulse and breathing; commence CPR if cardiac arrest

recallIt.question: What is the ABCD drug framework for treating hypertension, and which combinations should be avoided?
recallIt.answer:
A: ACE inhibitors / ARBs (lisinopril, losartan)
B: Beta-blockers (atenolol)
C: Calcium channel blockers (amlodipine)
D: Diuretics (indapamide)
Under 55: A → A+C → A+C+D → +alpha-blocker
Over 55 or African/Caribbean: C → A+C → A+C+D
Avoid B+D together: increases risk of new-onset diabetes

recallIt.question: What is the difference between amiodarone and lidocaine as antiarrhythmic drugs?
recallIt.answer: Lidocaine (Class I — Na channel blocker): used for ventricular arrhythmias; short half-life → IV infusion; causes hypotension and CNS toxicity. Amiodarone (Class III — K channel blocker): prolongs action potential duration; first-line for VF; very long half-life (contains iodine); serious adverse effects: thyroid disturbances, pulmonary fibrosis, peripheral neuropathy, hepatitis, irreversible blue-grey skin discolouration, pro-arrhythmia.

recallIt.question: What does Cor Pulmonale mean and what causes it?
recallIt.answer: Cor Pulmonale = right-sided heart failure caused by pulmonary hypertension from chronic lung disease (most commonly COPD). Chronically low oxygen → pulmonary vasoconstriction → raised pulmonary arterial pressure → right ventricle has to work harder → eventually fails. Presents with peripheral oedema, raised JVP, and fatigue.

recallIt.question: In order, what is the cardiac conducting system from pacemaker to ventricular muscle?
recallIt.answer: SA node (right atrium — sets rate 60–100 bpm) → atrial muscle (produces P wave on ECG) → AV node (delay = PR interval; gives time for atrial contraction to fill ventricles) → Bundle of His → right and left bundle branches → Purkinje fibres → ventricular muscle (produces QRS complex)

recallIt.question: Name the three layers of an arterial wall.
recallIt.answer:
1. Tunica intima — innermost; single layer of endothelial cells; site of atherosclerosis development
2. Tunica media — middle; smooth muscle and elastic fibres; maintains vessel tone
3. Tunica adventitia (externa) — outermost; connective tissue; anchors vessel

recallIt.question: Name six modifiable risk factors for cardiovascular disease.
recallIt.answer:
1. Hypertension (most important correctable risk factor; risk doubles every 20/10 mmHg rise)
2. Hyperlipidaemia (raised LDL, low HDL)
3. Smoking (carbon monoxide damages endothelium)
4. Diabetes mellitus
5. Obesity
6. Physical inactivity
Also: excessive alcohol, poor diet, psychosocial stress

recallIt.question: What is cardiac output, and what is the formula for it?
recallIt.answer:
Cardiac output (CO) = Stroke volume × Heart rate
Normal at rest: approximately 5 litres/minute (70 mL × 72 bpm)
Increases up to 25 L/min during intense exercise via increased heart rate and stroke volume (Starling's law)

recallIt.question: What is the difference between preload and afterload, and why do they matter in heart failure?
recallIt.answer:
Preload: the volume of blood stretching the ventricle at end of diastole (filling pressure). Increased by fluid overload/venous return.
Afterload: the resistance the heart must overcome to eject blood (arterial resistance). Increased by hypertension/vasoconstriction.
In heart failure: increased preload → pulmonary oedema; increased afterload → reduced stroke volume. ACE inhibitors and nitrates reduce both.

recallIt.question: Why do atherosclerotic plaques preferentially develop at arterial bifurcations?
recallIt.answer: At bifurcations, laminar blood flow becomes turbulent and creates low shear stress zones. This damages the endothelium mechanically, promoting LDL infiltration and monocyte adhesion. Common sites: coronary arteries, carotid bifurcation, femoral bifurcation, renal arteries, and the infrarenal aorta.

recallIt.question: Name the non-modifiable risk factors for cardiovascular disease.
recallIt.answer:
1. Age (>55 for men, >65 for women)
2. Male sex (oestrogen is cardioprotective pre-menopause)
3. Post-menopausal women (lose oestrogen protection)
4. Ethnicity (South Asian: higher IHD risk; African-Caribbean: higher hypertension/stroke risk)
5. Family history: first-degree relative with CVD under 55 (men) or 65 (women)
6. Genetics: familial hypercholesterolaemia, inherited thrombophilias

recallIt.question: What are the structural differences between arteries and veins?
recallIt.answer:
Arteries: thick muscular wall (large tunica media), no valves, high pressure, carry blood away from heart. Lumen relatively small compared to wall thickness.
Veins: thin wall, have valves (prevent backflow against gravity), low pressure, carry blood to heart. Large lumen relative to wall thickness.
Capillaries: one cell thick (5 microns), allow gas/nutrient exchange.

recallIt.question: Which coronary arteries supply which territories of the heart?
recallIt.answer:
Left anterior descending (LAD) artery: front (anterior wall) of left ventricle and interventricular septum — 'widowmaker'; occlusion causes anterior MI
Circumflex artery: lateral and posterior walls of left ventricle
Right coronary artery (RCA): inferior wall of heart, posterior descending artery, SA and AV nodes (most patients)
Right coronary artery territory MI → inferior MI on ECG (leads II, III, aVF)

recallIt.question: What is the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) and how does it raise blood pressure?
recallIt.answer:
Low renal perfusion → juxtaglomerular cells release renin → renin cleaves angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → ACE (in lungs) → angiotensin II → binds AT1 receptor → (1) aldosterone secretion from adrenal cortex → Na+/water retention → increased blood volume; (2) direct vasoconstriction → increased peripheral resistance; (3) sympathetic nervous system activation → tachycardia and vasoconstriction.
ACE inhibitors block this at the ACE step.

recallIt.question: Describe the four stages of atherosclerosis in order.
recallIt.answer:
1. Endothelial dysfunction: turbulent flow/risk factors damage endothelium at bifurcations
2. Fatty streak: monocytes enter intima → differentiate into macrophages → engulf LDL → foam cells (characteristic feature)
3. Fibrolipid (fibrous) plaque: smooth muscle cells migrate from media → secrete collagen → fibrous cap forms over lipid core; endothelium intact; lumen starts to narrow
4. Complicated lesion: endothelium stripped → platelets adhere → thrombus; calcification; haemorrhage into plaque; plaque fissure/rupture → embolism

recallIt.question: What is a 'vulnerable plaque' and why is it clinically dangerous?
recallIt.answer:
A vulnerable (unstable) plaque has:
- Large lipid pool
- Thin fibrous cap
- Many inflammatory macrophages (secrete matrix metalloproteinases that degrade the cap)
- Few smooth muscle cells
Dangerous because it is prone to rupture, exposing the lipid core → platelet activation and thrombosis → acute coronary syndrome or stroke. A stable plaque (thick cap, small lipid pool) is less likely to rupture despite causing greater stenosis.

recallIt.question: What is diapedesis and what role does it play in atherosclerosis?
recallIt.answer: Diapedesis is the passage of cells (especially monocytes/white cells) through the intact vessel wall by squeezing between endothelial cells. In atherosclerosis: endothelial dysfunction → monocytes adhere to damaged endothelium (via adhesion molecules VCAM-1, ICAM-1) → monocytes undergo diapedesis into the intima → differentiate into macrophages (under influence of lymphocyte cytokines) → engulf oxidised LDL → foam cells (fatty streak)

recallIt.question: What are lines of Zahn and what do they indicate?
recallIt.answer: Lines of Zahn are alternating pale bands (fibrin and platelets) and dark bands (red blood cells) seen histologically in a thrombus that formed in flowing blood during life (ante-mortem thrombus). The alternating layers form as clot builds up in flowing blood. They distinguish a true thrombus from a post-mortem clot (coagulum). Post-mortem clots have a homogeneous 'chicken fat' (yellow) and red jelly appearance without lines of Zahn.

recallIt.question: What is the role of LDL vs HDL cholesterol in atherosclerosis?
recallIt.answer:
LDL (low-density lipoprotein): harmful — deposits cholesterol in arterial walls; oxidised LDL in the intima is taken up by macrophages to form foam cells; raised LDL is a major risk factor for atherosclerosis.
HDL (high-density lipoprotein): protective — 'reverse cholesterol transport'; removes cholesterol from peripheral tissues and arterial walls and transports it to the liver for processing; high HDL is cardioprotective; low HDL is a CVD risk factor.
Target: LDL < 2 mmol/L in high-risk patients (statins achieve this).

recallIt.question: Name all three components of Virchow's triad with examples of each.
recallIt.answer:
1. Endothelial damage: hypertension, smoking, atherosclerosis, catheterisation, trauma
2. Abnormal blood flow: stasis (immobility, AF, venous thrombosis) or turbulence (bifurcations, stenosis)
3. Hypercoagulability: inherited (Factor V Leiden, protein C/S deficiency, antiphospholipid syndrome), acquired (malignancy, pregnancy, oestrogen therapy, nephrotic syndrome, polycythaemia)
All three are more relevant to venous thrombosis; endothelial damage and turbulence are more relevant to arterial thrombosis.

recallIt.question: What are the risk factors for deep vein thrombosis (DVT)?
recallIt.answer:
Virchow's triad components:
Stasis: immobility (long-haul flights, surgery, paralysis), congestive heart failure
Hypercoagulability: malignancy (Trousseau's sign), pregnancy, oestrogen (OCP, HRT), inherited thrombophilias, nephrotic syndrome
Endothelial damage: surgery, trauma, IV catheters
Other: increasing age, obesity, previous DVT, dehydration, burns
Risk scoring: Wells score for clinical probability before investigation

recallIt.question: What is a paradoxical embolism?
recallIt.answer: A paradoxical embolism is a venous thrombus that crosses from the right to the left circulation through a patent foramen ovale (PFO) or other septal defect, becoming a systemic arterial embolus. This can cause stroke or peripheral limb ischaemia in young patients without typical arterial risk factors. Approximately 25% of the population have a PFO. Cryptogenic (unexplained) stroke in young patients should prompt investigation for PFO with bubble echocardiography.

recallIt.question: What is haemopericardium and at what time point after MI is it most dangerous?
recallIt.answer: Haemopericardium = blood in the pericardial sac, usually from rupture of the necrotic ventricular wall following MI. Greatest risk: 4–6 days post-MI, when the infarcted myocardium is at its softest (maximum coagulative necrosis, before fibrosis). Blood fills the pericardial space → cardiac tamponade (compress heart → prevent filling → fall in cardiac output) → cardiogenic shock and sudden death. Treatment: urgent pericardiocentesis or surgical drainage.

recallIt.question: What is the difference between stable angina, unstable angina, and acute MI in terms of pathophysiology and symptoms?
recallIt.answer:
Stable angina: fixed atherosclerotic stenosis → reversible ischaemia on exertion; pain <5 min; resolves with rest/GTN; no troponin rise.
Unstable angina: plaque disruption → partial occlusion → ischaemia at rest or minimal exertion; pain >20 min; no troponin rise (no necrosis).
Acute MI: complete coronary occlusion → irreversible necrosis; pain at rest, severe, >20 min; troponin elevated (myocyte death releases it into bloodstream); ECG: ST changes.

recallIt.question: What is troponin and why is it the key biomarker in MI?
recallIt.answer: Troponin (troponin I and troponin T) is a regulatory protein in cardiac muscle fibres. It is highly specific to cardiac muscle (cTnI and cTnT isoforms unique to myocardium). When myocardial cells die (infarction), troponin leaks into the bloodstream. Levels rise within 3–6 hours of MI onset, peak at 24–48 hours, and remain elevated for up to 2 weeks. High sensitivity troponin assays allow earlier diagnosis. Serial measurements (at 0 and 3 hours) are used in emergency diagnosis.

recallIt.question: What is the difference between STEMI and NSTEMI?
recallIt.answer:
STEMI (ST-Elevation MI): complete occlusion of coronary artery → transmural (full thickness) infarction → ST elevation on ECG → emergency PCI (percutaneous coronary intervention) within 90 minutes ('door-to-balloon time') is treatment.
NSTEMI (Non-ST-Elevation MI): partial occlusion → subendocardial infarction → ST depression or T-wave changes (no ST elevation) → positive troponin → managed medically initially then early angiography. Both involve troponin rise (unlike unstable angina).

recallIt.question: What is the MONCA mnemonic for immediate MI management and what does each letter stand for?
recallIt.answer:
M — Morphine (IV, for pain and anxiety — also reduces catecholamine release)
O — Oxygen (15L/min via non-rebreathe mask if SaO2 <94%; NOT if normoxic — hyperoxia is harmful post-MI)
N — Nitrates (GTN sublingually — vasodilates, reduces preload)
C — Call 999
A — Aspirin 300mg chewed (irreversible COX inhibitor → antiplatelet → limits clot extension)
Also: clopidogrel (dual antiplatelet), anticoagulation (heparin/LMWH), and primary PCI.

recallIt.question: How does GTN (glyceryl trinitrate) spray work to relieve angina?
recallIt.answer:
GTN is a nitrate prodrug → metabolised to nitric oxide (NO) → activates guanylate cyclase in vascular smooth muscle → ↑cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation → vasodilation.
Effects: (1) Venodilation → reduces preload (less blood returning to heart) → reduced O2 demand; (2) coronary artery dilation → improves myocardial blood supply; (3) arteriodilation (at higher doses) → reduces afterload.
Dose: 2 puffs sublingually; repeat after 3–5 min if needed. Adverse effects: headache (meningeal vasodilation), flushing, hypotension. Nitrate tolerance requires 'nitrate-free period' (usually overnight).

recallIt.question: What drug classes are used for chronic stable angina and their mechanisms?
recallIt.answer:
1. Nitrates (GTN, isosorbide mononitrate) — NO donor → vasodilation → reduced preload + coronary dilation
2. Beta-blockers (atenolol, bisoprolol) — reduce heart rate and contractility → reduced O2 demand
3. Calcium channel blockers — dihydropyridines (amlodipine): vasodilate; rate-limiting (verapamil, diltiazem): slow HR + vasodilate
4. Nicorandil — K+ channel opener + nitrate → vasodilation; causes oral ulceration
5. Ivabradine — If channel blocker → reduces HR only (no effect on contractility); useful if beta-blockers not tolerated
6. Ranolazine — inhibits late Na+ current → reduces myocardial oxygen demand without affecting HR or BP

recallIt.question: When should elective dental treatment be deferred after an MI?
recallIt.answer:
Elective dental treatment should be deferred for at least 3–6 months after MI. During this period: the patient is at high risk of re-infarction; the autonomic nervous system is unstable; arrhythmia risk is high; and the patient may still be on new anticoagulant/antiplatelet regimens.
After 3–6 months (with cardiologist clearance if needed): routine treatment can proceed with: morning appointments, effective LA, minimal adrenaline, BP/ECG monitoring, GTN available.

recallIt.question: What is the time course of macroscopic changes in an MI?
recallIt.answer:
0–6 hours: no macroscopic change (but ECG changes present; troponin rises)
6–24 hours: slight pallor
24–48 hours: pallor with central yellow and haemorrhagic rim
3–5 days: pale, grey, soft (maximum softening → risk of rupture → haemopericardium)
1–2 weeks: grey, firm
Weeks to months: fibrosis → white scar replaces necrotic tissue (myocyte cannot regenerate)

recallIt.question: How do you manage an acute angina attack that occurs during dental treatment?
recallIt.answer:
1. Stop treatment immediately; remain calm
2. Sit patient upright (or in comfortable position — do not lie flat)
3. Administer patient's own GTN spray: 2 puffs sublingually
4. Wait 3–5 minutes
5. If unresolved: repeat GTN (2 more puffs)
6. If still unresolved after second dose: treat as MI — call 999; give 100% O2 15L/min; administer aspirin 300mg chewed; prepare to start CPR
7. Monitor pulse, respiration, and consciousness throughout
Do NOT administer GTN if patient is on phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil) — fatal hypotension risk

recallIt.question: What is the 'nitrate-free period' and why is it needed?
recallIt.answer:
Nitrate tolerance develops with continuous nitrate exposure: the enzyme converting organic nitrates to NO becomes depleted (sulfhydryl group depletion), and counter-regulatory neurohormonal mechanisms are activated. This reduces efficacy within 24–48 hours of continuous therapy.
To prevent tolerance: a nitrate-free period of 8–12 hours per day is built into dosing (e.g. isosorbide mononitrate taken at 8am and 2pm, not at night). GTN spray used intermittently does not develop tolerance. Asymmetric dosing is used for long-acting preparations.

recallIt.question: What are the major Duke criteria for diagnosing infective endocarditis?
recallIt.answer:
Major criteria (2 sufficient for definite IE):
1. Positive blood cultures: two separate cultures with typical IE organisms (viridans streptococci, S. aureus, HACEK); or persistently positive cultures with other organisms
2. Evidence of endocardial involvement on echocardiography: oscillating mass (vegetation) on valve; periannular abscess; new partial dehiscence of prosthetic valve; or new valvular regurgitation on echo
(Definite IE = 2 major; or 1 major + 3 minor; or 5 minor)

recallIt.question: What are the minor Duke criteria for infective endocarditis?
recallIt.answer:
Minor criteria:
1. Predisposing factor: cardiac condition or intravenous drug use
2. Fever >38°C
3. Vascular phenomena: arterial emboli, septic pulmonary infarcts, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial haemorrhage, conjunctival haemorrhages, Janeway lesions
4. Immunological phenomena: glomerulonephritis, Osler's nodes, Roth spots, positive rheumatoid factor
5. Microbiological evidence: positive blood culture not meeting a major criterion
Diagnosis: 2 major; or 1 major + 3 minor; or 5 minor = definite IE

recallIt.question: Describe the pathogenesis of rheumatic fever leading to valvular heart disease.
recallIt.answer: Group A Streptococcal pharyngitis → immune response produces antibodies against streptococcal M protein → molecular mimicry: antibodies cross-react with cardiac proteins (myosin, valvular proteins) → autoimmune inflammation of heart valves (Aschoff bodies histologically) → repeated episodes cause progressive valve scarring, thickening, fibrosis, and calcification → mitral stenosis (most common) or regurgitation. Rare now in UK due to penicillin treatment of strep throat. Still common in developing countries.

recallIt.question: What is the difference between tissue and mechanical prosthetic heart valves?
recallIt.answer:
Tissue (bioprosthetic) valves: made from porcine or bovine pericardium. Advantages: do NOT require long-term anticoagulation (aspirin only for 3 months post-op). Disadvantages: wear out over 10–15 years; need re-replacement. Used in: elderly patients, those who cannot tolerate anticoagulation.
Mechanical valves: metal (tilting disc or bileaflet). Advantages: last lifelong. Disadvantages: require LIFELONG warfarin anticoagulation (INR 2.5–4). Risk: IE, thromboembolism if anticoagulation inadequate. Used in: younger patients.

recallIt.question: Give three arguments against routine antibiotic prophylaxis for IE before dental procedures.
recallIt.answer:
1. No consistent evidence of causal link between dental procedures and IE — bacteraemia from dental procedures is transient and similar to daily toothbrushing
2. Cumulative bacteraemia from daily toothbrushing (365×/year) is far greater than a single dental procedure
3. Risk of antibiotic adverse effects (anaphylaxis, C. difficile colitis) may exceed the risk of preventing IE
4. Only a small fraction of IE cases are attributable to dental bacteraemia; most arise spontaneously
5. Poor evidence of efficacy from animal models — human RCTs impossible

recallIt.question: Which patients does SDCEP consider 'high risk' for infective endocarditis?
recallIt.answer:
High risk (SDCEP):
1. Previous infective endocarditis (highest risk)
2. Prosthetic heart valves or prosthetic material used for cardiac valve repair
3. Cyanotic congenital heart disease (unrepaired or with palliative shunt)
4. Repaired congenital heart disease with prosthetic material — first 6 months post-repair, or lifelong if residual shunt/regurgitation at site of prosthetic material

Moderate risk: rheumatic fever with valve damage, native valve disease, unrepaired non-cyanotic congenital defects

recallIt.question: What is TAVI (Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation) and who is it used for?
recallIt.answer:
TAVI is a catheter-based (keyhole) procedure for replacing a diseased aortic valve without open-heart surgery. A bioprosthetic valve mounted on a stent is compressed onto a catheter, inserted via the femoral artery (or directly through the chest in trans-apical approach), guided to the aortic valve position, and deployed by balloon inflation — pushing aside the diseased valve and anchoring in position.
Used for: aortic stenosis in high surgical risk or inoperable patients (elderly, multiple comorbidities). Transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (MitraClip) is analogous for mitral regurgitation.

recallIt.question: What is the NYHA classification of heart failure severity?
recallIt.answer:
Class I: No limitation — ordinary physical activity does not cause symptoms
Class II: Slight limitation — comfortable at rest; symptoms on moderate exertion (climbing stairs, walking on level)
Class III: Marked limitation — comfortable at rest; symptoms on minimal exertion (walking short distances)
Class IV: Unable to carry on any physical activity without discomfort; symptoms at rest; bed- or chair-bound
Relevant to dental management: NYHA III/IV require special precautions (positioning, shorter appointments, avoid supine position)

recallIt.question: What is BNP (B-type natriuretic peptide) and what is its clinical significance?
recallIt.answer:
BNP (B-type/Brain natriuretic peptide) and NT-proBNP are peptides released by ventricular myocytes in response to increased wall stretch (raised filling pressures). They promote natriuresis (sodium excretion) and vasodilation.
Clinical use: diagnostic biomarker for heart failure. Elevated BNP helps distinguish cardiac from respiratory cause of breathlessness. BNP is also used to monitor treatment response.
Normal BNP: <100 pg/mL (unlikely heart failure). Elevated: >400 pg/mL (high probability). Also used in prognosis — higher BNP = worse outcome.

recallIt.question: What is orthopnoea and why does it occur in left-sided heart failure?
recallIt.answer:
Orthopnoea = breathlessness on lying flat (requires multiple pillows to sleep comfortably). Occurs because: when supine, fluid from the lower limbs redistributes (hydrostatic pressure removed) → venous return to the heart increases → rises pulmonary blood volume → worsens pulmonary oedema → dyspnoea.
Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea: patient wakes from sleep acutely breathless 1–2 hours after lying down (fluid redistribution process takes time). Relieved by sitting upright. Dental implication: patients with orthopnoea cannot tolerate the supine dental chair position.

recallIt.question: What is the mechanism of action of furosemide and what are its electrolyte side effects?
recallIt.answer:
Mechanism: furosemide (frusemide) is a loop diuretic that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− co-transporter in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle → prevents reabsorption of sodium and water → 10-fold increase in urine output (most potent diuretic class).
Side effects:
- Hypokalaemia (most important — risk of arrhythmias; requires K+ monitoring/supplementation)
- Hyponatraemia
- Hypomagnesaemia
- Metabolic alkalosis
- Ototoxicity (especially with rapid IV administration or high doses)
- Gout (increases uric acid)
- Dehydration/hypovolaemia

recallIt.question: What is the mechanism of spironolactone and its two main adverse effects?
recallIt.answer:
Spironolactone: competitive aldosterone antagonist. Blocks aldosterone receptors in the collecting duct → prevents sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion (potassium-sparing diuretic). Also a pro-drug (active metabolite canrenone).
Benefits in heart failure: reduces fluid overload; improves survival (RALES trial — 30% mortality reduction).
Adverse effects:
1. Hyperkalaemia — dangerous; can cause fatal arrhythmias; avoid with ACE inhibitors + high potassium diet; monitor regularly
2. Gynaecomastia (and breast pain/impotence in men) — due to anti-androgen effects of spironolactone structure. Eplerenone (selective mineralocorticoid antagonist) causes less gynaecomastia.

recallIt.question: What are the signs and symptoms of digoxin toxicity?
recallIt.answer:
Digoxin has a narrow therapeutic index (therapeutic: 1–2 ng/mL; toxic: >2 ng/mL). Risk is increased in hypokalaemia (K+ competes with digoxin at Na/K-ATPase).
Signs/symptoms of toxicity:
- GI: nausea, vomiting, anorexia, diarrhoea
- Neurological: confusion, headache, fatigue, weakness
- Visual: xanthopsia (yellow-green tinge to vision), halos around lights, blurred vision
- Cardiac: bradycardia, heart block, atrial tachycardia with AV block, premature ventricular beats, VF
Management: stop digoxin; correct hypokalaemia; digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Digibind) for severe toxicity

recallIt.question: Why must beta-blockers be used only in STABLE, not ACUTE, heart failure?
recallIt.answer:
In stable chronic heart failure: beta-blockers reduce sympathetic over-stimulation of the failing heart → reduce myocardial damage and arrhythmias → improve survival (carvedilol, bisoprolol proven to reduce mortality by ~30%).
In acute/decompensated heart failure: the failing heart depends on sympathetic stimulation (tachycardia, increased contractility) to maintain cardiac output. Beta-blockers suddenly remove this compensatory drive → cardiac output falls → clinical deterioration, pulmonary oedema, hypotension. Therefore: NEVER start beta-blockers in acutely decompensated heart failure. Only introduce once patient is stable and euvolaemic.

recallIt.question: What is Cor Pulmonale and how does it develop?
recallIt.answer:
Cor Pulmonale = right ventricular failure (RVF) caused by pulmonary hypertension secondary to chronic lung disease (most commonly COPD).
Pathophysiology: chronic lung disease → chronic hypoxia → hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (the lung shunts blood away from poorly ventilated areas) → chronically raised pulmonary vascular resistance → right ventricle must pump against high afterload → right ventricular hypertrophy → eventually right ventricular failure.
Features: peripheral oedema, raised JVP, hepatomegaly, right parasternal heave. Cor Pulmonale differs from right heart failure caused by left heart disease.

recallIt.question: Summarise the Vaughan Williams classification of antiarrhythmic drugs with one example for each class.
recallIt.answer:
Class I — Sodium channel blockers: slow conduction; used for ventricular arrhythmias
  Ia: quinidine, procainamide (prolong action potential)
  Ib: lidocaine, mexiletine (shorten action potential)
  Ic: flecainide (minimal effect on action potential duration)
Class II — Beta-blockers (e.g. atenolol, metoprolol): reduce automaticity of SA node; used for AF rate control, SVT
Class III — Potassium channel blockers (e.g. amiodarone, sotalol): prolong action potential; used for VF, AF rhythm control
Class IV — Calcium channel blockers (e.g. verapamil, diltiazem): slow AV node conduction; used for SVT

recallIt.question: What is the mechanism of action and main adverse effects of amiodarone?
recallIt.answer:
Mechanism: Class III antiarrhythmic — potassium channel blocker → prolongs the cardiac action potential duration and refractory period → prevents re-entry arrhythmias; also has Class I, II, and IV effects.
First-line for: ventricular fibrillation (VF) resuscitation, haemodynamically unstable VT.
Adverse effects (extensive because amiodarone is highly lipophilic and accumulates in tissues):
- Thyroid: hypo- or hyperthyroidism (contains 37% iodine)
- Pulmonary fibrosis (most serious; dose-dependent)
- Peripheral neuropathy
- Hepatitis (LFT monitoring needed)
- Irreversible blue-grey skin discolouration (photosensitivity)
- Corneal microdeposits
- Pro-arrhythmic (can cause torsade de pointes)
Very long half-life (40–55 days)

recallIt.question: What is adenosine used for and how does it work?
recallIt.answer:
Adenosine is used to terminate acute supraventricular tachycardia (SVT), particularly those involving the AV node (e.g. AVNRT — AV nodal re-entry tachycardia, AVRT). It is also a diagnostic tool to reveal underlying atrial rhythm in tachycardias.
Mechanism: activates A1 adenosine receptors → opens K+ channels in the AV node → hyperpolarises the node → transiently blocks AV node conduction (seconds) → terminates re-entry circuits.
Administration: rapid IV bolus (6mg, then 12mg if needed). Very short half-life (<10 seconds). Adverse effects: transient flushing, chest tightness, brief asystole, bronchospasm (contraindicated in asthma).

recallIt.question: What is complete (third degree) heart block and how is it managed?
recallIt.answer:
Complete heart block (3rd degree): no impulses conducted from atria to ventricles through the AV node. Atria and ventricles beat independently (AV dissociation). Ventricular escape rhythm: 20–40 bpm (inherently slow, unstable, prone to asystole). 
Causes: MI (inferior), digoxin toxicity, fibrosis of conduction system, Lyme disease, cardiac surgery.
Symptoms: syncope (Stokes-Adams attacks), breathlessness, fatigue, sudden death.
Management: if haemodynamically compromised: atropine (IV) or temporary transcutaneous pacing immediately; followed by permanent pacemaker implantation (PPM).

recallIt.question: What is the risk of dental electromagnetic equipment for patients with pacemakers or ICDs?
recallIt.answer:
Older pacemakers were susceptible to electromagnetic interference (EMI) from dental equipment, which could cause pacemaker inhibition or reset. Modern pacemakers (post-2000) have better shielding and are generally safe.
Highest-risk dental equipment: ultrasonic scalers, electrosurgery/diathermy units, pulp testers, and older piezo devices.
Safest equipment: conventional dental drills, suction, X-rays, curing lights (all safe).
Management: check manufacturer pacemaker card; keep electrosurgery >15cm from device; if uncertain, avoid ultrasonic scalers and use alternative methods. ICD patients: same general principles.

recallIt.question: What is the CHA2DS2-VASc score and how is it used?
recallIt.answer:
CHA2DS2-VASc calculates stroke risk in atrial fibrillation to guide anticoagulation decisions:
C — Congestive heart failure (1)
H — Hypertension (1)
A2 — Age ≥75 (2 points)
D — Diabetes mellitus (1)
S2 — Stroke/TIA history (2 points — most important)
V — Vascular disease (IHD, PAD, aortic plaque) (1)
A — Age 65–74 (1)
Sc — Sex category: female (1)
Score ≥2 in males or ≥3 in females: anticoagulation recommended (warfarin or DOAC). Score 1 in males: consider anticoagulation. Score 0: no treatment needed.

recallIt.question: What is rate control vs rhythm control in atrial fibrillation?
recallIt.answer:
Rate control: reduce the ventricular rate to normal (60–100 bpm at rest) without trying to restore sinus rhythm. Drugs: beta-blockers (bisoprolol), digoxin, rate-limiting CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil). Often used in elderly patients or those with persistent AF.
Rhythm control: restore and maintain sinus rhythm. Methods: chemical cardioversion (amiodarone, flecainide), electrical DC cardioversion, catheter ablation. Used when rate control fails to control symptoms or in younger patients.
Anticoagulation: required for both strategies (if CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2) regardless of rhythm status.

recallIt.question: What is ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) and why is it used?
recallIt.answer:
ABPM: a portable device automatically records BP at regular intervals (usually every 30 minutes) over 24 hours as the patient goes about normal activities. NICE recommends ABPM as the gold standard for confirming hypertension diagnosis.
Why: avoids 'white coat hypertension' (elevated BP in clinic due to anxiety); detects 'masked hypertension' (normal in clinic, high at home); reveals nocturnal hypertension (non-dipping pattern associated with end-organ damage). ABPM threshold: daytime average ≥135/85 mmHg = Stage 1 hypertension (vs clinic ≥140/90).

recallIt.question: What are the adverse effects of ACE inhibitors relevant to dentistry?
recallIt.answer:
1. Dry cough (bradykinin accumulation — 10–15% of patients; reason to switch to ARB)
2. Angioedema (swelling of lips, tongue, larynx — potentially life-threatening airway emergency; rare but well-documented; can occur years after starting drug; treat with adrenaline IM, antihistamine, corticosteroid)
3. Hyperkalaemia (reduce K+ excretion — dangerous if combined with spironolactone or potassium-sparing diuretics)
4. Postural hypotension (vasodilation — patients may feel dizzy on sitting up after dental chair)
5. Altered taste (captopril — sulphydryl group; metallic taste)
6. Burning mouth syndrome (rarely reported)

recallIt.question: What is the FAST acronym for stroke recognition?
recallIt.answer:
F — Face: asymmetry, drooping, unilateral weakness; ask patient to smile
A — Arms: weakness; ask patient to raise both arms — one drifts downward
S — Speech: slurred, inappropriate words, unable to speak or understand
T — Time: note time of onset; call 999 immediately
Importance of time: ischaemic stroke treated with thrombolysis (alteplase) if within 4.5 hours; 'Time is brain' — approximately 1.9 million neurons die per minute of ischaemia. Door-to-needle time targets: thrombolysis within 60 minutes of hospital arrival.

recallIt.question: What is the difference between a TIA and a stroke in terms of definition and management?
recallIt.answer:
TIA (Transient Ischaemic Attack): focal neurological deficit due to vascular cause that resolves completely within 24 hours (by definition). Modern definition: resolves within 1 hour with no diffusion restriction on MRI.
Stroke: neurological deficit lasting >24 hours (or causing death) due to vascular cause — either ischaemic (85%) or haemorrhagic (15%).
Management of TIA: ABCD2 score (Age, BP, Clinical features, Duration, Diabetes) guides urgency. High-risk TIA: aspirin 300mg immediately; specialist review within 24 hours; antiplatelet, statin, antihypertensive. TIA has 10–15% stroke risk within 3 months.

recallIt.question: What is the ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) and how is it interpreted?
recallIt.answer:
ABPI = systolic BP at ankle ÷ systolic BP at arm (brachial artery). Measured with a handheld Doppler probe.
Interpretation:
>1.3: falsely elevated — calcified, incompressible arteries (common in diabetes, elderly); unreliable
0.9–1.3: normal
0.7–0.9: mild PAD — claudication
0.5–0.7: moderate PAD — claudication, consider revascularisation
<0.5: severe PAD — critical ischaemia likely; Doppler pressure in foot <50 mmHg indicates critical ischaemia
<0.3: limb at immediate risk of loss

recallIt.question: List the six Ps of acute limb ischaemia.
recallIt.answer:
1. Pain — severe, sudden onset
2. Pallor — cold, pale leg (white → blue → mottled)
3. Pulselessness — absent distal pulses
4. Paraesthesiae — numbness, tingling (ischaemia of sensory nerves)
5. Paralysis — loss of motor function (ischaemia of motor nerves)
6. Perishingly cold (poikilothermia) — limb assumes ambient temperature
Irreversible nerve and muscle damage occurs after 4–6 hours. Emergency management: IV heparin immediately, urgent analgesia, surgical embolectomy or bypass. Causes: thrombosis in situ (more common), arterial embolism (usually from AF or cardiac thrombus).

recallIt.question: What is aortic dissection, what causes it, and how does it present?
recallIt.answer:
Aortic dissection: a tear in the aortic intima allows blood to enter and track within the tunica media, creating a false lumen alongside the true lumen.
Causes: hypertension (most common — medial degeneration); connective tissue disorders (Marfan's syndrome — fibrillin-1 mutation; Ehlers-Danlos); trauma (road traffic accidents); coarctation; pregnancy (third trimester).
Presentation: sudden, severe, tearing or ripping chest pain radiating to the back/scapulae (different character from angina); unequal blood pressures in both arms; pulse deficit; hypertension or hypotension; aortic regurgitation murmur.
Management: emergency BP control (IV labetalol), surgical repair (Type A — involving ascending aorta) or stenting (Type B).

recallIt.question: What is a berry aneurysm and what is its classic complication?
recallIt.answer:
Berry (saccular) aneurysm: a sac-like outpouching of an arterial wall, typically at bifurcations of the circle of Willis (most common: junction of anterior communicating artery and anterior cerebral artery; posterior communicating artery is second most common). Associated with polycystic kidney disease, coarctation of aorta, connective tissue disorders.
Complication: rupture → subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH). Classic presentation: sudden severe 'thunderclap headache' ('worst headache of my life'), photophobia, meningism (neck stiffness, Kernig's sign). Diagnosis: CT head (blood in subarachnoid space) → lumbar puncture if CT negative (xanthochromia). Management: neurosurgical clipping or endovascular coiling.

recallIt.question: How does heparin work and how is it reversed?
recallIt.answer:
Mechanism: heparin enhances the activity of antithrombin III (a natural anticoagulant) by 1000-fold → antithrombin III inactivates thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa → immediate anticoagulation. Unfractionated heparin (UFH) inhibits both thrombin and Xa. LMWH (dalteparin, enoxaparin) preferentially inhibits factor Xa.
Monitoring: UFH monitored by APTT (activated partial thromboplastin time); LMWH does not need routine monitoring (predictable dose response).
Reversal: protamine sulphate neutralises UFH rapidly (but only partially reverses LMWH). Dose: 1mg protamine per 100 units of heparin administered in last 2–3 hours.

recallIt.question: What is the difference between unfractionated heparin (UFH) and low molecular weight heparin (LMWH)?
recallIt.answer:
UFH: large molecule; IV or SC; inhibits thrombin AND factor Xa; monitored by APTT; short half-life (1–2 hrs); fully reversible with protamine; risk of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT); used in hospital, renal failure, when rapid reversal needed.
LMWH (dalteparin, enoxaparin, tinzaparin): fragments of heparin; SC injection once or twice daily; predominantly inhibit factor Xa; predictable pharmacokinetics → no routine monitoring; less HIT risk; longer half-life (4–6 hrs); renally excreted (contraindicated in severe renal failure); partially reversed by protamine; easier outpatient use.

recallIt.question: What is the mechanism of warfarin and which clotting factors does it inhibit?
recallIt.answer:
Warfarin: vitamin K antagonist — inhibits hepatic vitamin K epoxide reductase enzyme → prevents recycling of vitamin K back to its active form → depletes vitamin K-dependent clotting factors.
Factors inhibited: II (prothrombin), VII, IX, X (mnemonic: 1972 — these were discovered in 1972) and protein C and protein S (anticoagulants — important: early in warfarin therapy, protein C falls first → brief hypercoagulable state → reason for heparin overlap when starting warfarin).
Monitoring: INR (International Normalised Ratio — standardises PT ratio; normal = ~1; therapeutic = 2–3 for most; 2.5–3.5 for mechanical valves).
Half-life: 35–37 hours; 98% protein-bound.

recallIt.question: What is the mechanism of action of clopidogrel and what is its dental significance?
recallIt.answer:
Clopidogrel: prodrug (requires hepatic CYP2C19 activation to active thiol metabolite) → irreversible antagonist of P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets → prevents ADP-induced platelet aggregation.
Onset: 3–7 days for full effect. Duration: irreversible for platelet lifespan (7–10 days).
Dental significance: increases bleeding risk (like aspirin). For routine extractions: continue clopidogrel — do NOT stop (risk of in-stent thrombosis in coronary stent patients can be fatal). Use local haemostatic measures. If on dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel after PCI): both must be continued — stopping either increases coronary stent thrombosis risk significantly.

recallIt.question: What are the reversal agents for the four main DOACs?
recallIt.answer:
DOACs and reversal:
1. Dabigatran (thrombin inhibitor) → reversed by idarucizumab (Praxbind — humanised antibody fragment; binds dabigatran with very high affinity; approved 2015)
2. Rivaroxaban / apixaban / edoxaban (factor Xa inhibitors) → reversed by andexanet alfa (FXa decoy protein; approved 2019); also coagulation factor concentrates (PCC — 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate) can be used in emergencies
For non-emergency procedures: no reversal agent needed; time the procedure according to DOAC half-life and renal function (usually skip one dose or treat in morning before dose). Activated charcoal if within 2 hours of ingestion.

recallIt.question: What drugs can cause gingival hyperplasia and what is the management?
recallIt.answer:
Drugs causing gingival overgrowth:
1. Calcium channel blockers — dihydropyridines: nifedipine (most common), amlodipine (less common)
2. Ciclosporin (calcineurin inhibitor/immunosuppressant — post-organ transplant)
3. Phenytoin (anti-epileptic)
Pathophysiology: increased collagen synthesis by fibroblasts; reduced collagen degradation; poor oral hygiene exacerbates severity.
Management: intensive oral hygiene instruction (reduces severity); referral to periodontist; liaison with physician about drug substitution (e.g. switch from nifedipine to amlodipine or to a different antihypertensive class — some improvement but may not fully resolve); surgical gingivectomy as last resort if functional impairment.

recallIt.question: What are the oral manifestations of drugs used in cardiovascular disease?
recallIt.answer:
Gingival hyperplasia: calcium channel blockers (dihydropyridines — nifedipine, amlodipine)
Xerostomia: beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, diuretics, CCBs
Oral ulceration: nicorandil (severe, non-healing, can be extensive)
Lichenoid reactions: beta-blockers, diuretics, ACE inhibitors, thiazide diuretics, NSAIDs, statins
Angioedema (tongue/lip swelling): ACE inhibitors — emergency!
Altered taste: captopril (metallic taste), atorvastatin
Increased tooth demineralisation: beta-blockers (reduce salivary flow + alter saliva composition)
Burning mouth syndrome: ACE inhibitors (occasionally)
Periodontal disease (bidirectional relationship): CVD shares inflammatory pathways with periodontitis

recallIt.question: Why are morning appointments preferred for patients with significant cardiovascular disease?
recallIt.answer:
Cortisol (endogenous steroid) peaks in the morning, providing natural cardiovascular protection and better stress tolerance. Most adverse cardiac events (MI, sudden cardiac death, stroke, arrhythmias) peak in the early to mid-morning (6am–noon) due to morning sympathetic surge, platelet aggregability, and catecholamine levels.
For dental patients with CVD: morning appointments → patient arrives at their most physiologically robust; afternoon/evening appointments coincide with fatigue and can be harder to manage. Shorter appointments reduce stress. Effective LA is critical — pain and anxiety cause equal catecholamine surges to the physical stress of surgery.

recallIt.question: In which cardiovascular conditions are vasoconstrictors (adrenaline) in LA relatively or absolutely contraindicated?
recallIt.answer:
Absolutely contraindicated or use minimum amount:
- Uncontrolled severe hypertension (systolic >200 or diastolic >115 mmHg)
- Unstable angina or recent MI (<3 months)
- Uncontrolled cardiac arrhythmias
- Severe uncontrolled heart failure
- Phaeochromocytoma (catecholamine-secreting tumour — risks hypertensive crisis)
- Patients on MAOIs or tricyclic antidepressants (potentiate adrenaline)

Relatively contraindicated (use minimal amounts with aspiration and slow injection):
- Controlled hypertension
- Well-controlled heart failure
- Non-selective beta-blockers (theoretical risk of hypertension from unopposed alpha stimulation — usually safe in practice)

recallIt.question: What post-operative analgesia is safest in patients with heart failure, and what should be avoided?
recallIt.answer:
Safe:
- Paracetamol (first-line — no cardiovascular effects; safe up to 1g QDS in normal hepatic function)
- Codeine/dihydrocodeine (weak opioids — second-line; monitor for constipation)

AVOID:
- NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac, naproxen, COX-2 inhibitors): cause sodium and water retention (worsen oedema and heart failure); reduce renal perfusion (especially dangerous with ACE inhibitors — 'triple whammy': ACE inhibitor + diuretic + NSAID → acute renal failure); increase blood pressure; increase cardiovascular event risk
- Aspirin at analgesic doses (325mg+) — same prostaglandin-mediated effects on kidney and fluid balance

recallIt.question: What is the periodontal–cardiovascular disease link?
recallIt.answer:
Bidirectional inflammatory relationship between periodontitis and CVD:
Evidence: multiple meta-analyses show 1.5–2× increased CVD risk in patients with periodontal disease, independent of shared risk factors (smoking, diabetes).
Proposed mechanisms:
1. Bacteraemia: periodontal bacteria (Porphyromonas gingivalis, Tannerella forsythia) enter bloodstream → adhere to atherosclerotic plaques → promote platelet aggregation and thrombus formation
2. Systemic inflammation: raised CRP, IL-6, TNF-alpha from chronic periodontal infection → promotes endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerosis progression
Clinical relevance: treating periodontal disease may reduce systemic CRP and improve endothelial function. Regular dental review is part of CVD prevention.

recallIt.question: How should you position a patient with significant left-sided heart failure in the dental chair?
recallIt.answer:
DO NOT place in supine (fully flat) position. Orthopnoea means the patient cannot lie flat without becoming breathless.
Position: semi-reclined or upright (at least 30–45° from horizontal; ideally treat in the position the patient can breathe most comfortably). Ask the patient before starting ('How many pillows do you sleep on?').
Rationale: supine position increases venous return to the heart → increases pulmonary blood volume → worsens pulmonary oedema → acute breathlessness. Sitting up uses gravity to shift fluid to dependent areas (legs) away from lungs.
