mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the mechanism by which felypressin (octapressin) — an alternative vasoconstrictor to adrenaline in some LA formulations — works?
options: A. Felypressin is a synthetic catecholamine that stimulates both alpha and beta adrenoceptors
B. Felypressin is a synthetic vasopressin (ADH) analogue that acts on V1 receptors in vascular smooth muscle, causing vasoconstriction without beta-adrenoceptor effects — no direct cardiac effects
C. Felypressin inhibits nitric oxide synthesis in blood vessels, preventing vasodilation at the injection site
D. Felypressin activates alpha-1 receptors exclusively in gingival vessels but has no effect on coronary or peripheral arteries
E. Felypressin blocks adenosine receptors to prevent vasodilation at the injection site
mockBank.explanation: Felypressin (also known as octapressin) is a synthetic analogue of vasopressin (antidiuretic hormone/ADH). It acts on V1 receptors on vascular smooth muscle cells → phospholipase C activation → IP3/DAG pathway → intracellular Ca2+ release → smooth muscle contraction → vasoconstriction. Unlike adrenaline, felypressin has NO beta-adrenoceptor activity — no direct cardiac stimulation (no increase in HR, contractility, or arrhythmic risk). Available in Citanest with Octapressin (prilocaine 3% + felypressin 0.03 IU/mL). Used as an alternative in patients where adrenaline is relatively contraindicated (e.g. thyrotoxicosis, severe uncontrolled hypertension). Note: felypressin can cause uterine contractions — relatively contraindicated in pregnancy.

mockBank.question: A 58-year-old man has known peripheral arterial disease and hypertension. He is started on an ACE inhibitor (ramipril). Two weeks later, his creatinine has risen by 45% (from 88 to 128 µmol/L). Which ONE of the following BEST explains this rise?
options: A. Ramipril is directly nephrotoxic and should always be stopped if creatinine rises by any amount

B. A rise in creatinine ≤30% is expected and acceptable when starting ACE inhibitors in most patients — it reflects haemodynamic changes; a rise >30–50% should prompt investigation for bilateral renal artery stenosis
C. The creatinine rise indicates ramipril is successfully improving glomerular filtration and is a positive response to treatment
D. Ramipril should be continued regardless of creatinine rise — ACE inhibitors are renoprotective in all clinical scenarios
E. This creatinine rise is caused by muscle wasting from ACE inhibitor-induced myopathy
mockBank.explanation: ACE inhibitors cause a predictable and acceptable rise in creatinine of up to 20–30% when started — this reflects a reduction in GFR from reduced efferent arteriolar tone (Ang II normally constricts the efferent arteriole; blocking this reduces glomerular filtration pressure). This is haemodynamic, not structural, and is acceptable. A rise >30–50%, or in the context of PAD (which raises suspicion of concurrent renal artery stenosis — renovascular hypertension), requires investigation. Bilateral renal artery stenosis (or stenosis of a solitary kidney) is a contraindication to ACE inhibitors — efferent constriction is critical to maintaining GFR in these patients; removing it causes acute kidney injury (significant creatinine rise).

mockBank.question: A 67-year-old man with controlled hypertension on atenolol attends for a routine check-up. The dentist notices a white reticulate pattern on both buccal mucosae bilaterally, with occasional erosions. The patient is unaware of the lesions. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Perform incisional biopsy immediately as this is suspicious for oral squamous cell carcinoma
B. Diagnose as oral lichen planus; prescribe chlorhexidine mouthwash; discharge

C. Recognise this as a likely drug-induced lichenoid reaction to atenolol; photograph; consider biopsy to confirm; liaise with GP about possible drug substitution; referral to oral medicine if symptomatic
D. Reassure the patient that this is normal variation in the oral mucosa and requires no follow-up
E. Prescribe a course of oral antifungals as this is likely oral candidosis masquerading as lichen planus
mockBank.explanation: A bilateral white reticulate pattern on the buccal mucosae is consistent with oral lichen planus or a drug-induced lichenoid reaction. Beta-blockers (atenolol, propranolol) are among the most common drugs causing oral lichenoid reactions, which are clinically and histologically indistinguishable from idiopathic OLP. Management: document and photograph the lesion; biopsy to confirm the diagnosis histologically (dense sub-epithelial lymphocytic infiltrate at the basement membrane zone); liaise with the GP to discuss substituting atenolol with a non-beta-blocker antihypertensive. If the lesion resolves after drug withdrawal, the diagnosis of lichenoid drug reaction is confirmed. Refer to oral medicine for erosive/symptomatic lesions requiring topical corticosteroid therapy.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes 'myocardial stunning' after a brief episode of myocardial ischaemia?
options: A. Permanent loss of myocardial function from irreversible necrosis
B. Transient but prolonged reversible myocardial dysfunction persisting after an episode of ischaemia, despite restoration of coronary flow and absence of irreversible damage
C. A form of angina that occurs at rest and is caused by vasospasm in non-obstructed coronary arteries
D. Chronic hibernation of myocardium due to severely reduced resting coronary blood flow
E. The phenomenon of ST elevation persisting for more than 24 hours after MI without myocardial damage
mockBank.explanation: Myocardial stunning: after brief ischaemia (too short to cause irreversible injury) followed by reperfusion, the myocardium may remain dysfunctional for hours to days despite normal blood flow and no necrosis. The function eventually fully recovers. Mechanism: reactive oxygen species generated during reperfusion ('reperfusion injury'), calcium overload, and altered contractile protein sensitivity. Clinical importance: after primary PCI for STEMI, ventricular function may initially be reduced due to stunning — it recovers over days to weeks. Echocardiographic assessment immediately post-reperfusion underestimates final LV function. Myocardial hibernation (option D) is chronic dysfunction from chronically reduced (but not absent) coronary flow — also reversible with revascularisation.

mockBank.question: A patient on warfarin (INR 2.4) is admitted with a large spontaneous haematoma and haemoglobin has fallen from 130 to 88 g/L. He is haemodynamically stable. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate management?
options: A. Give oral vitamin K 5mg and transfuse packed red cells if needed; repeat INR in 24 hours
B. No treatment required as the INR is within the therapeutic range
C. Give IV vitamin K 10mg immediately to fully reverse warfarin before assessing further
D. Administer IV fresh frozen plasma (FFP) and 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) for rapid warfarin reversal; consider stopping warfarin long-term
E. Restart warfarin at double the dose to compensate for the blood loss
mockBank.explanation: This patient has serious haemorrhage (haemoglobin fallen 42 g/L with spontaneous haematoma). For serious warfarin-associated bleeding: (1) Immediate warfarin reversal using 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC — Beriplex, Octaplex) which rapidly (within minutes) replenishes factors II, VII, IX, X — PREFERRED over FFP (slower, larger volume, risk of transfusion reactions); (2) IV vitamin K 5–10mg — effect takes 6–12 hours but prevents rebound re-anticoagulation as PCC effect wanes; (3) Red cell transfusion if haemodynamically significant anaemia. FFP alone is slower and requires large volumes. The cause of bleeding and future anticoagulation need must be reassessed before restarting warfarin.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is the CORRECT classification of NYHA class III heart failure?
options: A. No limitation — ordinary physical activity causes no undue fatigue or dyspnoea
B. Slight limitation — comfortable at rest; ordinary activity causes fatigue or dyspnoea
C. Marked limitation — comfortable at rest; less than ordinary activity causes fatigue or dyspnoea
D. Symptoms at rest — unable to perform any physical activity without discomfort
E. Symptoms only during maximal exertion — normal daily activities are unaffected
mockBank.explanation: NYHA functional classification: Class I — No symptoms; ordinary activity unaffected. Class II — Slight limitation; comfortable at rest; ordinary activity causes mild symptoms. Class III — Marked limitation; comfortable at rest; LESS THAN ordinary activity (e.g. walking on level ground, light housework) causes symptoms. Class IV — Symptoms at rest; unable to carry out any physical activity without discomfort. NYHA class determines prognosis and guides treatment decisions (e.g. CRT indicated in NYHA II–IV; ICD risk reduction starts at class I with EF <35%; transplant listing at class IV refractory to therapy).

mockBank.question: A 55-year-old woman with severe mitral regurgitation (EF 40%) is asymptomatic. Her LV end-systolic dimension is 45mm (threshold for surgery is 45mm). Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Reassure her and review annually with no intervention — all asymptomatic MR can be managed conservatively
B. Surgical mitral valve repair is recommended even in asymptomatic patients when specific thresholds are met (EF ≤60% or LVESD ≥45mm)
C. Start ACE inhibitor therapy — this will reduce the severity of MR and avoid the need for surgery
D. Wait until symptoms develop before considering surgery — asymptomatic patients always have better outcomes by waiting
E. Prescribe prophylactic anticoagulation with warfarin as all severe MR patients require anticoagulation
mockBank.explanation: Mitral regurgitation management: in asymptomatic patients, surgery is recommended when echocardiographic thresholds are met, indicating incipient LV decompensation before symptoms develop. ESC/ACC/AHA thresholds for class I surgical indication in asymptomatic MR: (1) EF ≤60% — contractile dysfunction is already occurring; (2) LVESD ≥45mm — LV enlargement from volume overload approaching irreversible dilation. This patient meets both thresholds (EF 40%, LVESD 45mm) — mitral valve repair (preferred over replacement — better survival, avoids anticoagulation) is indicated. Surgical MR repair results are excellent when performed before irreversible LV dysfunction. ACE inhibitors do not reduce primary MR severity.

mockBank.question: A 45-year-old woman is found to have BP 160/100 mmHg at two separate visits. ABPM confirms daytime average 155/96 mmHg. She has no target organ damage. Her 10-year cardiovascular risk (QRISK3) is 8%. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Lifestyle advice only — pharmacological treatment is not required until QRISK3 exceeds 20%
B. Stage 2 hypertension — offer lifestyle advice AND antihypertensive drug treatment (ACE inhibitor or CCB depending on age/ethnicity)
C. Refer urgently to cardiology — stage 2 hypertension at age 45 always requires specialist assessment
D. Start two antihypertensives immediately — single drug therapy is never sufficient for stage 2 hypertension
E. No treatment required — BP up to 160/100 mmHg is acceptable in a 45-year-old woman without symptoms
mockBank.explanation: NICE classification: Stage 2 hypertension = clinic BP ≥160/100 mmHg (confirmed on ABPM ≥150/95 mmHg). Management: offer antihypertensive drug treatment REGARDLESS of cardiovascular risk (contrast with stage 1 hypertension where QRISK3 ≥10% is required). Lifestyle advice simultaneously (diet, exercise, weight, smoking, alcohol). First-line drug: under 55 years white British = ACE inhibitor or ARB; over 55 or African/Caribbean = CCB. No target organ damage or high cardiovascular risk means referral to specialist is NOT required — manage in primary care. Start with single drug and uptitrate/add further drugs as needed to achieve target (<140/90 mmHg, or <130/80 mmHg in diabetes/CKD).

mockBank.question: A 35-year-old man presents with syncope during vigorous exercise. His ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy with deep septal Q-waves in leads I, V5–V6. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: A. Dilated cardiomyopathy from myocarditis — ventricular dilation and reduced EF
B. Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) — asymmetric septal hypertrophy causing LVOT obstruction; most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes
C. Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) — right-sided cardiomyopathy with fatty replacement of RV myocardium
D. Long QT syndrome — exercise-triggered events with a prolonged QTc on ECG
E. Cardiac sarcoidosis — granulomatous infiltration causing LVH and conduction abnormalities
mockBank.explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM): autosomal dominant mutation in sarcomeric proteins (MYH7 — beta-myosin heavy chain most common; MYBPC3). Asymmetric septal hypertrophy → dynamic LVOT obstruction (worsened by exercise — increases HR, reduces ventricular filling, reduces afterload). ECG: deep Q-waves in lateral/inferior leads (septal depolarisation abnormality); LVH voltage; ST-T abnormalities. Symptoms: exertional dyspnoea, angina, palpitations, syncope/pre-syncope. Sudden cardiac death from VF/VT during exercise (most common cause of sudden death in young athletes in Europe). Management: avoid competitive sports; beta-blockers/verapamil for symptoms; ICD for sudden death prevention in high-risk patients; septal reduction therapy (TASH or surgical myectomy) for LVOTO.

mockBank.question: A patient with AF and CKD stage 3 (eGFR 42 mL/min/1.73m²) needs anticoagulation. Which ONE of the following DOACs is considered the SAFEST choice in moderate renal impairment?
options: A. Dabigatran — 80% renally excreted; most dependent on renal clearance — avoid in significant CKD
B. Rivaroxaban — 33% renal excretion; requires dose reduction in CKD; once daily
C. Apixaban — 27% renal excretion; least renally dependent DOAC; approved down to eGFR 15 mL/min (with dose reduction at 25mg BD threshold)
D. Edoxaban — 50% renal excretion; dose reduction required; not recommended below eGFR 15
E. All DOACs are equally safe in all stages of CKD — no dose adjustment is ever required
mockBank.explanation: DOAC renal excretion: dabigatran 80% > edoxaban 50% > rivaroxaban 33% > apixaban 27%. For CKD: dabigatran is most problematic (contraindicated when eGFR <30); rivaroxaban and edoxaban require dose reductions at lower eGFR thresholds; apixaban (least renally dependent) can be used down to eGFR 15 mL/min with appropriate dose adjustment (standard 5mg BD reduced to 2.5mg BD if ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60kg, creatinine ≥133 µmol/L — apixaban dose-reduction criteria). For eGFR 42, all DOACs can technically be used, but apixaban provides the greatest safety margin for further renal decline. Always check current SmPC/BNF for specific dose-adjustment thresholds.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the physiological basis of the baroreceptor reflex in blood pressure regulation?
options: A. Baroreceptors in the aortic body detect changes in blood oxygen saturation and adjust heart rate accordingly

B. Stretch-sensitive baroreceptors in the aortic arch and carotid sinus detect BP changes; increased stretch → increased afferent signals → brainstem reduces sympathetic outflow and increases vagal tone → BP falls
C. Baroreceptors in the kidney glomerulus detect changes in renal perfusion pressure and regulate renin release
D. Baroreceptors in the right atrium detect increased venous return and trigger ADH release to prevent volume overload
E. Baroreceptors in the peripheral chemoreceptors detect CO2 changes and adjust heart rate to maintain pH
mockBank.explanation: The baroreceptor reflex (baroreflex): stretch-sensitive mechanoreceptors in the aortic arch (aortic baroreceptors — afferents via vagus nerve X) and carotid sinus (afferents via glossopharyngeal nerve IX) detect arterial wall stretch proportional to BP. Increased BP → increased afferent firing → nucleus tractus solitarius in medulla → reduced sympathetic outflow (↓HR, ↓contractility, ↓SVR) + increased parasympathetic/vagal outflow (↓HR via AV node slowing) → BP falls back toward normal. Decreased BP → opposite response → increased sympathetic output → ↑HR, ↑contractility, ↑SVR → BP rises. The baroreflex provides moment-to-moment BP regulation. It is chronically 'reset' upward in hypertension.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the management of a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA)?
options: A. Urgent CT angiogram to confirm size and morphology, then elective repair the following week
B. Medical management with IV antihypertensives and bed rest until the aneurysm stabilises
C. Emergency surgical repair (open or EVAR) — ruptured AAA is a surgical emergency with 50–80% overall mortality; permissive hypotension pre-operatively (target SBP 50–70 mmHg) to reduce blood loss
D. Transfer to the nearest medical ward for monitoring and urgent haematology consultation
E. Thrombolysis with alteplase to dissolve the thrombus within the aneurysm sac
mockBank.explanation: Ruptured AAA: catastrophic haemorrhage from the aorta → exsanguination. Mortality >50% overall (including pre-hospital deaths); in-hospital mortality even with emergency surgery ~40%. Management: (1) Call for emergency surgical team — there is no time for detailed imaging if the patient is haemodynamically compromised; (2) IV access, blood products; (3) Permissive hypotension pre-operatively (SBP 50–70 mmHg) — excessive resuscitation worsens bleeding by disrupting retroperitoneal haematoma tamponade; (4) Emergency repair — endovascular (EVAR — favoured if anatomy permits, less physiological insult) or open surgery (if anatomy unsuitable for EVAR). CT angiogram if stable; direct to OR if unstable. Every minute of delay increases mortality.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the mechanism of action of protamine sulphate in reversing heparin?
options: A. Protamine activates antithrombin III more strongly than heparin, displacing heparin from its binding site
B. Protamine is a strongly positively charged protein that binds to the negatively charged heparin molecule, forming an inactive complex, thereby neutralising its anticoagulant activity
C. Protamine inhibits factor Xa directly, correcting the anticoagulation caused by heparin
D. Protamine stimulates hepatic production of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X to overcome the heparin effect
E. Protamine binds to antithrombin III and prevents heparin from activating it
mockBank.explanation: Protamine sulphate is derived from salmon sperm. It is a strongly positively charged protein (+) that forms a stable ionic complex with negatively charged (-) heparin molecules → the protamine-heparin complex has no anticoagulant activity. 1mg protamine neutralises approximately 100 units of UFH. Protamine completely reverses UFH; partially reverses LMWH (only the anti-IIa component — approximately 60–70% of anti-Xa activity is not reversed). It does NOT reverse DOACs (idarucizumab reverses dabigatran; andexanet alfa reverses factor Xa inhibitors). Adverse effects: hypotension, pulmonary vasoconstriction, anaphylaxis (risk higher in patients previously exposed to protamine-containing insulin or fish allergy).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following dental procedures is MOST likely to cause a clinically relevant bacteraemia with viridans group streptococci?
options: A. Taking a bitewing radiograph — incidental contact with the gingival cuff causes transient bacteraemia
B. Placement of a rubber dam clamp — slight gingival displacement causes bacteraemia equivalent to extraction
C. Tooth extraction with socket irrigation — breaching the periodontal ligament and periapical vasculature releases oral bacteria into the bloodstream
D. Applying fluoride varnish to the tooth surfaces — fluoride ions cause local tissue damage and bacteraemia
E. Impression taking with alginate — the material compresses the gingivae causing bacteraemia identical to scaling
mockBank.explanation: Bacteraemia magnitude from dental procedures (highest to lowest): tooth extraction > surgical procedures > scaling/root planing > endodontic treatment beyond the apex > matrix band/wedge placement. Tooth extraction breaches the periodontal ligament, tears the gingival attachment, and traumatises the highly vascular periapical tissues — releasing oral bacteria into the bloodstream at high concentrations. However, it is important to note that toothbrushing causes cumulative bacteraemia exceeding the total from all dental procedures over a year — hence why oral hygiene (not antibiotic prophylaxis) is the most effective IE prevention strategy. Viridans streptococci (Streptococcus mitis, S. sanguinis, S. oralis) are the predominant organisms released during dental procedures.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly explains why symptoms of left ventricular failure (pulmonary oedema) can occur without physical signs of fluid overload in some patients?
options: A. Left heart failure always causes peripheral oedema before pulmonary symptoms develop

B. In acute LV failure (e.g. acute MI causing sudden MR), pulmonary oedema develops before the kidneys have time to retain sodium and water — there may be no peripheral oedema or raised JVP in early acute LVF
C. Left heart failure causes redistribution of existing fluid from the abdomen to the lungs without increasing total body water
D. Pulmonary oedema in LV failure is caused by lymphatic obstruction rather than excess fluid — the total body water is normal
E. Right heart failure must always accompany left heart failure before pulmonary oedema develops
mockBank.explanation: Acute LVF (flash pulmonary oedema) vs. chronic heart failure: In acute LVF (from sudden LV dysfunction — e.g. anterior STEMI, acute MR from chordal rupture), LVEDP and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure rise suddenly. Pulmonary oedema develops within minutes to hours from hydrostatic pressure forcing fluid into alveoli. At this early stage, there has been insufficient time for the kidneys to activate the RAAS and retain sodium/water → no peripheral oedema, JVP may not be markedly elevated, no ankle swelling. The total body fluid is not significantly increased yet. This contrasts with chronic HF where sodium/water retention causes peripheral oedema AND pulmonary congestion. Recognising acute pulmonary oedema with minimal peripheral signs is clinically important.

mockBank.question: A 70-year-old man is started on digoxin for AF. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important drug interaction to monitor when he is prescribed co-amoxiclav for a dental infection?
options: A. Co-amoxiclav inhibits CYP3A4, raising digoxin plasma levels and causing toxicity
B. Co-amoxiclav kills gut bacteria that normally convert digoxin to reduced metabolites in the colon — this can raise plasma digoxin levels by approximately 10–30% in approximately 10% of patients
C. Co-amoxiclav directly displaces digoxin from Na/K-ATPase binding sites, reducing its cardiac effect
D. Co-amoxiclav is absolutely contraindicated in patients on digoxin — always use metronidazole instead
E. There is no clinically significant interaction between co-amoxiclav and digoxin
mockBank.explanation: In approximately 10% of patients, gut bacteria (principally Eubacterium lentum) convert significant amounts of digoxin to inactive reduced metabolites in the colon — reducing systemic bioavailability. When broad-spectrum antibiotics (co-amoxiclav, erythromycin, clarithromycin, tetracycline) eliminate these bacteria, this conversion is impaired → more digoxin is absorbed systemically → plasma digoxin levels rise by 10–30% → potential toxicity. This interaction is relevant for approximately 1 in 10 patients on digoxin. If antibiotics are prescribed, digoxin toxicity should be monitored (GI symptoms, visual changes, ECG changes). Erythromycin and clarithromycin also inhibit P-glycoprotein (which excretes digoxin renally/intestinally), compounding the interaction.

mockBank.question: A 55-year-old man is diagnosed with a transient ischaemic attack (TIA). Which ONE of the following is the MOST important early investigation to determine the mechanism and guide secondary prevention?
options: A. 24-hour Holter monitor immediately to exclude paroxysmal AF as the cause
B. Carotid duplex ultrasound as the single most important investigation — carotid artery stenosis is the most common TIA cause

C. Brain MRI (DWI), 12-lead ECG, carotid duplex ultrasound, fasting glucose/lipids, and FBC — a comprehensive battery of investigations to identify the mechanism (cardioembolic, large vessel, small vessel, or haematological)
D. Lumbar puncture to exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage mimicking TIA
E. CT head with contrast as the first-line investigation — identifies both haemorrhage and ischaemia simultaneously
mockBank.explanation: After TIA, the cause must be identified to guide secondary prevention: (1) Brain MRI DWI — confirms ischaemia vs non-ischaemic mimic (tumour, demyelination, metabolic); (2) 12-lead ECG — screens for AF (cardioembolic); (3) Carotid duplex ultrasound — identifies carotid stenosis (>50% stenosis → urgent carotid endarterectomy within 2 weeks); (4) 24-hour Holter (and extended monitoring if ECG negative) — detects paroxysmal AF; (5) Echocardiogram if cardioembolic cause suspected; (6) Blood tests (FBC, glucose, lipids, coagulation). Secondary prevention depends on mechanism: carotid stenosis → CEA + antiplatelet + statin; AF → anticoagulation; small vessel → antiplatelet + BP control + statin.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the difference between a 'Type I' and 'Type II' endoleak after endovascular aortic aneurysm repair (EVAR)?
options: A. Type I — blood leaking from the graft fabric itself (graft porosity); Type II — blood entering the aneurysm sac from collateral vessels (lumbar or IMA)
B. Type I — attachment site leak (proximal or distal inadequate seal between graft and native aorta); Type II — retrograde flow from patent side branches (lumbar arteries, IMA) into the aneurysm sac
C. Type I — rupture of the aneurysm despite EVAR; Type II — graft migration causing aortic dissection
D. Type I — early endoleak (within 30 days); Type II — delayed endoleak (after 30 days)
E. Both types are identical — the classification merely reflects the location (proximal vs. distal) of the same leak type
mockBank.explanation: Endoleak classification after EVAR: Type I — attachment site leak — inadequate seal between the stent-graft and the native aortic wall at the proximal (Ia) or distal (Ib) neck. Blood flows at systemic pressure into the aneurysm sac — HIGH PRESSURE leak requiring urgent intervention (extension cuff, conversion to open repair). Type II — the most common endoleak — blood enters the aneurysm sac retrograde from patent side branches: lumbar arteries or inferior mesenteric artery. Usually a LOW PRESSURE leak; many seal spontaneously; intervention if sac is expanding. Type III — graft fabric defect/disconnection (treat urgently). Type IV — graft porosity. Type V — 'endotension' — sac expansion without identifiable leak.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly identifies the therapeutic drug monitoring parameter used for warfarin and explains why it is used rather than a direct drug level measurement?
options: A. Activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) — used for warfarin because it reflects activity of the intrinsic pathway which warfarin primarily affects
B. International Normalised Ratio (INR) — standardises the prothrombin time across different laboratory reagents/thromboplastins; reflects activity of factors II, VII, IX, X which warfarin inhibits
C. Anti-Xa level — directly measures warfarin's inhibition of factor Xa; the most sensitive test for warfarin monitoring
D. Thrombin time — measures thrombin activity; directly reflects warfarin's inhibitory effect on prothrombin
E. Plasma warfarin concentration — a direct drug level that correlates perfectly with anticoagulant effect
mockBank.explanation: The INR (International Normalised Ratio) = (patient PT / mean normal PT)^ISI, where ISI is the International Sensitivity Index of the laboratory thromboplastin reagent. The prothrombin time (PT) reflects the activity of the extrinsic and common pathway clotting factors — particularly factor VII (shortest half-life, falls first on warfarin) and factors II, IX, X. Standardisation with ISI corrects for variations between different thromboplastin reagents used in different laboratories — ensuring that a result in one lab means the same as in another. Anti-Xa levels monitor LMWH and DOACs (not warfarin). aPTT monitors UFH (not warfarin). Direct plasma warfarin levels do not correlate well with anticoagulant effect due to PK variability.

mockBank.question: A 73-year-old woman with heart failure has an echocardiogram showing an ejection fraction of 52% with evidence of increased left ventricular filling pressures. BNP is elevated. Which ONE of the following diagnosis and classification is MOST appropriate?
options: A. HFrEF — EF of 52% is below the normal threshold of 60%
B. HFpEF (Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction) — EF ≥50%, symptoms/signs of HF, and evidence of raised filling pressures (diastolic dysfunction)
C. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy — preserved EF with diastolic dysfunction always indicates HCM
D. Cardiac amyloidosis — the only condition causing raised filling pressures with preserved EF
E. This echocardiogram is entirely normal — no treatment is required
mockBank.explanation: HFpEF (Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction): EF ≥50%, clinical features of HF (dyspnoea, exercise intolerance, fluid retention), and objective evidence of cardiac dysfunction causing elevated filling pressures — demonstrated by raised BNP/NT-proBNP AND echocardiographic evidence of diastolic dysfunction (elevated E/e' ratio, enlarged LA, abnormal deceleration time). HFpEF is increasingly common — particularly in elderly women with hypertension, obesity, and diabetes. The diastolic filling abnormality is not from reduced systolic function but from impaired myocardial relaxation and increased LV stiffness. HFmrEF ('mid-range EF') = EF 40–49%. HFrEF = EF <40%.

mockBank.question: A 66-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history presents with acute anterior STEMI. She receives primary PCI within 75 minutes with successful stent placement in the mid-LAD. Her EF 24 hours later is 38%. Which ONE of the following outpatient medications has demonstrated mortality benefit and should be prescribed?
options: A. Aspirin + ticagrelor + high-intensity statin + beta-blocker + ACE inhibitor
B. Aspirin alone — single antiplatelet therapy is sufficient after PCI with drug-eluting stent
C. Warfarin + aspirin — dual antithrombotic therapy is required after stent insertion
D. Only a statin — statins alone have proven mortality benefit; antiplatelet therapy is optional after 30 days
E. Aspirin + clopidogrel + digoxin + warfarin — the four mandatory drugs post-MI
mockBank.explanation: Post-STEMI/PCI evidence-based secondary prevention (all have mortality benefit in this context): (1) Aspirin 75mg lifelong — antiplatelet, reduces re-infarction; (2) Ticagrelor 90mg BD (or prasugrel/clopidogrel) for 12 months — DAPT prevents in-stent thrombosis (drug-eluting stent) and reduces MACE; (3) High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80mg) — reduces LDL, plaque stabilisation, pleiotropic effects; (4) Beta-blocker (bisoprolol/metoprolol) — reduces arrhythmias, remodelling, sudden death; (5) ACE inhibitor (ramipril) — reduces cardiac remodelling, prevents HF progression (especially with reduced EF 38%). All five drugs are class I recommendations post-STEMI with reduced EF. Add MRA (eplerenone) if EF ≤40% with clinical HF signs (further mortality benefit — EPHESUS trial).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes 'nitrate tolerance' and how it is prevented?
options: A. Nitrate tolerance is an allergic reaction to nitrates that develops after 3 doses and prevents further GTN use

B. Nitrate tolerance is the loss of haemodynamic effectiveness of long-acting nitrates from continuous exposure — prevented by providing a nitrate-free interval of 8–12 hours daily (typically overnight)
C. Nitrate tolerance refers to the need for increasing doses to maintain the same analgesic effect — similar to opioid tolerance
D. Nitrate tolerance is caused by nitric oxide oxidising haemoglobin to methaemoglobin, reducing oxygen delivery
E. Nitrate tolerance develops after a single dose of GTN and prevents any subsequent benefit from the drug
mockBank.explanation: Nitrate tolerance: with continuous/prolonged exposure to organic nitrates (isosorbide mononitrate, isosorbide dinitrate), haemodynamic effects (vasodilation, BP reduction, symptom relief) diminish over hours. Mechanisms: (1) Depletion of mitochondrial aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH2) — the enzyme that biotransforms nitrates to NO; (2) Increased reactive oxygen species neutralising NO; (3) Neurohormonal counter-regulation (sympathetic activation, RAAS). Prevention: eccentric dosing — prescribe long-acting nitrates twice daily with doses taken at 8am and 3pm (provides an 8–12 hour nitrate-free window overnight when nitrates are absent). Isosorbide mononitrate 60mg (modified-release, once daily) is formulated to provide a nitrate-free period in its pharmacokinetic profile.

mockBank.question: A 65-year-old patient on warfarin (INR 2.3) has a dry socket (alveolar osteitis) 4 days after extraction. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Stop warfarin before treating the dry socket — any intervention in an anticoagulated patient is too risky
B. Gently irrigate with normal saline and pack with Alvogyl (or equivalent non-eugenol preparation); do not disturb the socket excessively; do not alter warfarin therapy
C. Refer to hospital immediately as dry socket treatment is contraindicated in anticoagulated patients
D. Prescribe metronidazole 400mg three times daily — it is the treatment of choice for alveolar osteitis in warfarin patients
E. Incise and drain the socket under local anaesthetic, then pack with iodoform gauze to promote healing
mockBank.explanation: Dry socket (alveolar osteitis) in an anticoagulated patient: management is the same as in non-anticoagulated patients — gentle saline irrigation to remove food debris from the socket, placement of an analgesic/antiseptic dressing (Alvogyl, BIPP, Whitehead's varnish gauze, or medicated ribbon gauze). Warfarin must NOT be stopped — the thromboembolism risk from stopping outweighs any marginal benefit for dry socket treatment. Metronidazole should be avoided in warfarin patients due to the dangerous CYP2C9 interaction causing INR elevation. If infection is present (pain, swelling, systemic features), amoxicillin (or cefalexin if penicillin-allergic) is a safer antibiotic choice. INR check is not required for dry socket dressing — it is a non-invasive dressing procedure.

mockBank.question: A 68-year-old woman with AF is being discharged from hospital. Her cardiologist plans to use flecainide as 'pill in the pocket' therapy. Which ONE of the following conditions MUST be excluded before this strategy can be safely employed?
options: A. Previous history of anxiety disorder — flecainide can worsen anxiety in susceptible patients
B. Structural heart disease (coronary artery disease, LV dysfunction, significant hypertrophy) — flecainide is pro-arrhythmic in structural heart disease and MUST be excluded first
C. Renal failure — flecainide is exclusively renally cleared and is always contraindicated in CKD
D. Previous thyroid disease — thyroid hormones alter flecainide metabolism
E. Sleep apnoea — flecainide causes nocturnal AF storms in untreated OSA
mockBank.explanation: 'Pill in the pocket' (PoP) flecainide: the patient takes a dose of flecainide (200–300mg as a single oral dose) when symptomatic AF begins — to chemically cardiovert rapidly without needing hospitalisation. Safety requirement: the patient MUST have no structural heart disease (no significant coronary artery disease, no LV dysfunction, no significant LVH). The CAST trial demonstrated flecainide (Class Ic) INCREASED mortality in patients post-MI with ventricular ectopy — the drug's pro-arrhythmic effect causes sustained VT/VF in diseased myocardium. Therefore, PoP flecainide is only safe in patients with 'lone AF' (structurally normal heart confirmed by echocardiography and exercise testing). An AV node-blocking drug (beta-blocker or diltiazem/verapamil) must also be taken with flecainide to prevent 1:1 flutter conduction.

mockBank.question: A 55-year-old man with hypertension is prescribed bendroflumethiazide (a thiazide diuretic). He returns 6 months later with acute podagra (gout) of the right first metatarsophalangeal joint. Which ONE of the following BEST explains this complication?
options: A. Thiazides cause hyperuricaemia by increasing uric acid production in the liver
B. Thiazides decrease renal uric acid excretion by competing for the same tubular secretion pathway — uric acid accumulates in plasma and precipitates as monosodium urate crystals in joints
C. Thiazides cause hyperuricaemia by inhibiting xanthine oxidase, the enzyme that converts xanthine to uric acid
D. Thiazides directly cause gout by stimulating neutrophil infiltration into joints independent of uric acid levels
E. The gout is unrelated to bendroflumethiazide — the patient's diet has changed
mockBank.explanation: Thiazide diuretics cause hyperuricaemia by reducing renal uric acid excretion: thiazides increase uric acid reabsorption in the proximal tubule and decrease secretion — both effects increase plasma uric acid levels. The mechanism involves competition for urate transporters (OAT1/OAT3) in the proximal tubule. Elevated plasma urate → saturation → monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints (particularly the first MTP joint — podagra) and soft tissues (tophi). Management options for thiazide-induced gout: switch to an alternative antihypertensive if gout is recurrent/severe (e.g. amlodipine — CCBs have a mild uricosuric effect); treat acute gout with colchicine or NSAIDs (short course); allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor) for prophylaxis if gout recurs despite antihypertensive change.

mockBank.question: A 38-year-old man is found to have a bicuspid aortic valve on echocardiography performed for a murmur. His echocardiogram also shows aortic root dilation of 4.0cm with no significant stenosis or regurgitation. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management plan?
options: A. Immediate surgical aortic root replacement — 4.0cm aortic root is an emergency requiring surgery today
B. Annual echocardiographic surveillance; surgical aortic root repair when dimension reaches 5.0–5.5cm or growth >5mm/year or other high-risk features; beta-blockers or ARBs to slow aortic dilation
C. Discharge with no follow-up — bicuspid aortic valve is a minor finding and does not require monitoring
D. Start warfarin immediately — all patients with bicuspid aortic valve require anticoagulation to prevent valve thrombosis
E. Prescribe high-dose statins — reducing LDL will slow the progression of bicuspid valve stenosis
mockBank.explanation: Bicuspid aortic valve (BAV) is the most common congenital heart defect (1–2% of population). Complications: accelerated aortic stenosis (develops 20 years earlier than tricuspid valve AS), aortic regurgitation, and — crucially — aortic root/ascending aorta dilation (aortopathy — increased risk of dissection/rupture independent of valve function). Surveillance: annual echo if aorta >4.0cm. Surgical indication (ascending aorta repair): ≥5.5cm (elective); ≥5.0cm if BAV with other risk factors (Marfan, family history of dissection, planned cardiac surgery). Beta-blockers (reduce aortic wall stress) or ARBs (losartan — evidence in Marfan/BAV for slowing dilation). Also: screen first-degree relatives with echo. Statins have no evidence of slowing BAV stenosis progression.

mockBank.question: A patient on losartan (an ARB) for hypertension develops bilateral lower limb oedema that has progressively worsened over 3 months. His GP suspects drug-induced oedema. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely culprit?
options: A. Losartan — ARBs commonly cause ankle oedema as a class effect

B. The patient is also on amlodipine — calcium channel blockers (especially dihydropyridines) cause dose-dependent ankle/leg oedema from arteriolar dilation without corresponding venodilation → capillary hydrostatic pressure increase → fluid leak into interstitium
C. The patient is also on ramipril — ACE inhibitors directly cause salt and water retention leading to oedema
D. The patient is also on bisoprolol — beta-blockers cause oedema by reducing cardiac output and increasing venous back pressure
E. Losartan combined with any antihypertensive always causes peripheral oedema due to drug interactions
mockBank.explanation: Calcium channel blocker (CCB) — particularly dihydropyridine CCB (amlodipine, nifedipine) — induced peripheral oedema is the most common cause of drug-induced ankle/leg oedema. Mechanism: dihydropyridines dilate precapillary arterioles (reducing resistance) but have relatively less effect on venules/veins → increased capillary hydrostatic pressure → fluid moves into interstitial compartment → oedema. Not caused by fluid retention (the oedema does not respond to diuretics) — it is gravitational/positional fluid redistribution. Management: reduce CCB dose, switch to rate-limiting CCB (diltiazem/verapamil — less oedema but cannot combine with beta-blocker), or add an ACE inhibitor/ARB (which causes venodilation and reduces CCB-induced oedema). ARBs and ACE inhibitors do not typically cause oedema.

mockBank.question: A patient asks about taking St John's Wort (hypericum perforatum) herbal supplement for depression. She is on warfarin for AF (INR 2.5). Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate advice?
options: A. St John's Wort is a natural product and has no interactions with warfarin
B. St John's Wort induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, significantly REDUCING warfarin plasma levels and the INR — there is a risk of thromboembolic stroke; avoid combination
C. St John's Wort inhibits CYP2C9 and will raise the INR dangerously — avoid with warfarin
D. St John's Wort interacts only with SSRIs and has no effect on warfarin metabolism
E. St John's Wort can be taken safely alongside warfarin provided the dose is under 300mg/day
mockBank.explanation: St John's Wort (hypericum perforatum) is one of the most important herbal medicine-drug interactions. It is a potent inducer of CYP3A4, CYP2C9 (less so), and P-glycoprotein. Inducing CYP enzymes accelerates warfarin metabolism → reduced plasma warfarin levels → sub-therapeutic INR → increased risk of thromboembolic stroke. Multiple case reports document strokes in AF patients who started St John's Wort while on warfarin. The BNF explicitly warns against this combination. Patients on warfarin should be advised to avoid ALL herbal supplements unless specifically discussed with their anticoagulant clinic. The same CYP induction effect reduces levels of many other drugs (ciclosporin, antiretrovirals, oral contraceptives, DOACs).

mockBank.question: During a dental appointment, a patient's ECG monitor shows a sudden flat-line rhythm and the patient loses consciousness. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate first action?
options: A. Apply the AED immediately and shock as the flat line represents VF
B. Check the monitor leads — a flat line is most commonly an ECG artefact (dislodged lead); verify by checking the patient clinically (airway, breathing, pulse)
C. Call 999 immediately without assessing the patient
D. Administer IV adrenaline 1mg as the flat line represents pulseless electrical activity
E. Deliver a precordial thump to convert the rhythm
mockBank.explanation: A flat line on an ECG monitor does NOT automatically mean asystole. The first step is to verify: (1) Check the patient — are they responsive, breathing, have a pulse? (2) Check monitor leads — a dislodged electrode is the most common cause of a flat line on a monitor; (3) Increase the ECG gain/sensitivity — fine VF can appear as a flat line at low gain. If the patient is unresponsive, not breathing normally, no pulse → cardiac arrest is confirmed → call 999, start CPR, attach AED. Do NOT shock until VF/pVT is confirmed on the AED analysis — asystole is NOT shockable. The AED will correctly identify the rhythm. Precordial thump is no longer recommended in shockable rhythms (no evidence of benefit) and is never used for asystole.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the concept of 'myocardial hibernation' and its clinical significance?
options: A. Myocardial hibernation is irreversible — hibernating myocardium cannot recover function even after revascularisation

B. Myocardial hibernation describes chronically dysfunctional but viable myocardium in regions of severely reduced (but not absent) resting coronary blood flow — function can recover after revascularisation
C. Myocardial hibernation is identical to myocardial stunning — both describe the same post-ischaemic phenomenon
D. Myocardial hibernation refers to reduced metabolic activity during sleep, which is a normal physiological phenomenon
E. Hibernating myocardium is identified by its inability to take up fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) on PET scanning
mockBank.explanation: Myocardial hibernation: a state of chronic, reversible myocardial dysfunction caused by persistent coronary hypoperfusion at rest (severely stenosed but not occluded artery). The myocardium 'hibernates' — downregulates its metabolic and contractile function to match the reduced oxygen supply — avoiding irreversible injury. Crucially, this tissue is VIABLE and can recover contractile function if coronary blood flow is restored by revascularisation (PCI or CABG). Clinical importance: patients with poor LV function may have significant hibernating myocardium that would benefit from revascularisation. Assessment: dobutamine stress echo (low-dose dobutamine stimulates contractile reserve in hibernating muscle) or FDG-PET (hibernating myocardium has preserved glucose uptake despite reduced flow — 'flow-metabolism mismatch').

mockBank.question: A 65-year-old man with known PAD undergoes successful femoral-popliteal bypass using a synthetic (PTFE) graft. Three months later he develops an acutely ischaemic leg. Imaging shows the graft is occluded. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely mechanism of graft occlusion?
options: A. Anastomotic false aneurysm from infection of the synthetic graft material
B. Neointimal hyperplasia at the distal anastomosis — smooth muscle cell proliferation from surgical trauma and turbulent flow narrows the lumen, predisposing to thrombosis
C. Progression of proximal atherosclerosis above the graft origin, obstructing inflow
D. Spontaneous resolution of the underlying PAD removing the pressure gradient through the graft
E. Immunological graft rejection — the body treats PTFE as a foreign protein and destroys it
mockBank.explanation: Early bypass graft failure (within 1 year): most commonly caused by neointimal hyperplasia at the distal anastomosis. The surgical trauma of anastomosis + turbulent flow at the anastomotic site → smooth muscle cell and fibroblast proliferation into the intima of the vessel/graft → progressive luminal narrowing → graft thrombosis. This is why autologous vein grafts (saphenous vein) have better long-term patency than synthetic (PTFE/Dacron) grafts — endothelialised surface with less neointimal hyperplasia. Graft surveillance with duplex ultrasound at regular intervals (3, 6, 12 months) detects developing anastomotic stenoses that can be treated before occlusion. Early failure (<30 days) is more commonly technical. Late failure (>1 year) is more commonly progressive atherosclerosis.

mockBank.question: A 72-year-old man on apixaban for AF presents to the dental emergency clinic with a peri-apical abscess causing facial swelling and fever. He requires urgent extraction. He took his morning dose of apixaban at 8am, and it is now 2pm (6 hours later). Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Wait 24 hours until the apixaban has fully cleared before performing the extraction
B. Administer andexanet alfa before the extraction to ensure haemostasis
C. Proceed with extraction; continue apixaban; use effective haemostasis (packing, sutures, tranexamic acid mouthwash)
D. Prescribe antibiotics only; defer extraction until anticoagulant clinic advice is sought
E. Check anti-Xa level first; only proceed if it is in the sub-therapeutic range
mockBank.explanation: An acute dental infection with facial swelling and fever is a dental emergency that requires extraction — deferral risks worsening infection and spread. For urgent extraction in a patient on apixaban: proceed without stopping apixaban (stopping risks stroke from AF). Six hours after the 8am dose, some anti-Xa effect remains but is less than peak (peak is at 3–4 hours). Use local haemostatic measures: pack socket with oxidised cellulose; suture the socket; prescribe tranexamic acid 5% mouthwash 10mL four times daily for 2 days. Anti-Xa level monitoring is not part of routine dental decision-making. Andexanet alfa is reserved for life-threatening haemorrhage. Deferring an acute infection treats the anticoagulant concern but ignores the more immediate patient safety issue (spreading infection).

mockBank.question: A 52-year-old man is found to have a total cholesterol of 7.2 mmol/L, HDL 0.9 mmol/L, LDL 5.4 mmol/L, and triglycerides 2.1 mmol/L. His QRISK3 is 14%. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the treatment approach?
options: A. Lifestyle advice only for 12 months before considering statin therapy as QRISK3 <20%
B. Offer high-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 20–80mg) as QRISK3 ≥10% meets the threshold for pharmacological primary prevention
C. Prescribe a fibrate (fenofibrate) as elevated triglycerides indicate that statin therapy is contraindicated
D. No treatment is required — total cholesterol of 7.2 mmol/L is within the acceptable range for men
E. Refer urgently to a lipid clinic as LDL >5 mmol/L requires specialist management before any treatment can commence
mockBank.explanation: NICE guideline: offer statin therapy to individuals with a QRISK3 10-year cardiovascular risk of ≥10%. At QRISK3 14% with LDL 5.4 mmol/L and low HDL (0.9 mmol/L — a significant cardiovascular risk factor), high-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 20mg for primary prevention — lower dose than secondary prevention due to benefit/risk considerations) is indicated after lifestyle discussion. The target in primary prevention is ≥40% reduction in non-HDL cholesterol from baseline. Fibrates are primarily used for severe hypertriglyceridaemia (>10 mmol/L — pancreatitis risk) or as add-on therapy; they do not replace statins. LDL 5.4 mmol/L should also raise suspicion of familial hypercholesterolaemia (FH) — formal FH criteria assessment is warranted.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the 'vulnerable period' of the cardiac cycle and its significance for arrhythmia generation?
options: A. The vulnerable period is the P-R interval — stimulation during this period causes AF
B. The vulnerable period is the peak of the T-wave (relative refractory period) — a PVC occurring during this period ('R-on-T phenomenon') can trigger ventricular fibrillation
C. The vulnerable period is the QRS complex — PVCs during QRS depolarisation cause bundle branch re-entry
D. The vulnerable period is the ST segment — ischaemia during this phase specifically triggers atrial flutter
E. The vulnerable period occurs only in patients with prolonged QT syndrome
mockBank.explanation: The cardiac vulnerable period corresponds to the peak of the T-wave — specifically the relative refractory period of the ventricular action potential when not all cells have fully repolarised. During this period, the cells are in different refractory states (some excitable, some not) — creating electrical inhomogeneity. A premature ventricular contraction (PVC) occurring during this window ('R-on-T' phenomenon) can encounter cells in different refractory states → functional re-entry → sustained VT or VF. The R-on-T phenomenon is particularly dangerous in the setting of myocardial ischaemia (which lengthens and disperses the refractory period) and long QT syndrome (further extending the vulnerable period). This concept underpins why synchronised cardioversion is timed to the R-wave — to avoid the T-wave vulnerable period.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following medications used in stroke secondary prevention specifically reduces the risk of haemorrhagic transformation of an ischaemic stroke?
options: A. Aspirin 300mg daily — its antiplatelet effect prevents haemorrhagic transformation
B. Warfarin — anticoagulation prevents all post-stroke complications including haemorrhage
C. Clopidogrel — more potent antiplatelet than aspirin, better prevents haemorrhagic transformation
D. No medication specifically prevents haemorrhagic transformation — it is managed by avoiding anticoagulants and controlling BP in the acute phase
E. IV alteplase (thrombolysis) — dissolves microthrombi preventing haemorrhagic transformation
mockBank.explanation: Haemorrhagic transformation (HT) of ischaemic stroke: breakdown of the blood-brain barrier in the infarcted zone allows red cell extravasation into the ischaemic tissue. Risk factors: large infarct, cardioembolic mechanism, anticoagulation, thrombolysis, reperfusion. Prevention: (1) Avoid anticoagulants in the acute phase (typically delay starting anticoagulation for 1–2 weeks post-ischaemic stroke, longer if large infarct); (2) Strict BP control (target <180 mmHg in the acute phase); (3) No pharmacological agent specifically prevents HT. Aspirin (option A) is started early after ischaemic stroke for secondary prevention but does NOT reduce HT risk — it actually marginally increases it. Anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs) is delayed to reduce HT risk.

mockBank.question: A patient on bisoprolol (a cardioselective beta-blocker) for hypertension develops an acute asthma attack. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management of the asthma attack in this context?
options: A. Do not give any bronchodilators — beta-2 agonists are absolutely contraindicated in patients on beta-blockers
B. Give IV adrenaline 500 micrograms immediately — the only effective bronchodilator in the presence of beta-blockade
C. Administer salbutamol (beta-2 agonist) via inhaler or nebuliser; consider IV aminophylline if response is poor; do not stop bisoprolol abruptly during the acute attack
D. Immediately stop bisoprolol — this will allow full beta-2 receptor recovery and resolve the bronchospasm within 30 minutes
E. Treat with oral prednisolone alone — beta-agonists are ineffective in the presence of any beta-blocker
mockBank.explanation: Beta-agonists (salbutamol) should be given for acute asthma even in patients on beta-blockers. While cardioselective beta-blockers (bisoprolol, atenolol) partially block beta-2 receptors, high-dose inhaled salbutamol can still achieve bronchodilation by overcoming the competitive blockade. Salbutamol remains first-line; the dose may need to be higher than usual. IV aminophylline (methylxanthine — bronchodilator via different mechanism) or IV magnesium can be added if response is suboptimal. Bisoprolol must NOT be stopped abruptly (rebound tachycardia, hypertension, angina risk) — the acute asthma takes priority and the bisoprolol issue is addressed electively. IV adrenaline risks uncontrolled hypertension in a patient with partial beta-2 blockade (unopposed alpha-1 vasoconstriction).

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly identifies the most important preventative measure against infective endocarditis in at-risk dental patients?
options: A. Antibiotic prophylaxis before every dental procedure that may cause bacteraemia
B. Maintenance of excellent oral hygiene and regular professional dental care to minimise cumulative bacteraemia from daily oral hygiene activities
C. Restricting dental treatment to only emergency procedures in high-risk IE patients
D. Prescribing prophylactic anticoagulation to prevent valve thrombus formation that could harbour bacteria
E. Replacing all natural teeth with implant-supported prostheses to eliminate the periodontal ligament space where bacteria accumulate
mockBank.explanation: The NICE guideline on prophylaxis against infective endocarditis (2016) concludes that the cumulative bacteraemia from daily toothbrushing far exceeds the bacteraemia from all dental procedures combined over a lifetime. Therefore, the most effective strategy against IE is maintaining excellent oral hygiene: (1) regular twice-daily toothbrushing; (2) regular professional scaling and periodontal care; (3) prompt treatment of dental disease; (4) regular dental attendance. This reduces the daily burden of oral bacteria entering the bloodstream. NICE explicitly does not recommend routine antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures for any patients. High-risk patients (prosthetic valves, previous IE) should be educated about oral hygiene and IE symptoms.

mockBank.question: A 58-year-old woman with a family history of coronary artery disease (father died of MI aged 55) has a calculated QRISK3 score of 7%. She has no other cardiovascular risk factors. Which ONE of the following is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Prescribe atorvastatin 20mg immediately as family history alone is a treatment indication
B. Reassure and discharge — QRISK3 7% is below the 10% treatment threshold; lifestyle advice given; recheck in 5 years
C. Arrange CT coronary calcium score (CAC) — if CAC score is elevated (e.g. >100), this reclassifies her to higher risk and treatment may be appropriate despite QRISK3 <10%
D. Prescribe aspirin 75mg for primary prevention given her family history
E. Refer to cardiology as all patients with family history of premature CAD require specialist assessment
mockBank.explanation: When clinical risk assessment is borderline (QRISK3 7–10%), coronary CT calcium score (CAC) can 'reclassify' risk. A high CAC score (e.g. Agatston >100) indicates significant subclinical atherosclerosis — the patient is at higher actual risk than QRISK3 suggests. This may tip the decision toward statin therapy. A zero CAC score at age 58 effectively rules out significant subclinical atherosclerosis and supports deferring pharmacotherapy. NICE guidance allows for CAC scoring in borderline-risk patients to refine the decision. Aspirin for primary prevention is NOT recommended (excess bleeding risk without adequate benefit in primary prevention — ASCEND trial). Family history of premature CVD (first-degree relative, male <55, female <65) IS included in QRISK3 as a risk factor.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly identifies the clinical situation where the combination of verapamil AND digoxin is particularly dangerous?
options: A. In patients with WPW syndrome and AF — verapamil slows AV node conduction, forcing conduction via the accessory pathway at very high rates, potentially causing VF
B. In patients with hypertension — combining verapamil and digoxin causes severe hypertension
C. In patients with heart failure — both drugs reduce heart failure symptoms but cause hyperkalaemia
D. In dental patients receiving LA with adrenaline — verapamil blocks the vasoconstrictive effect of adrenaline
E. In patients over 70 — elderly patients cannot metabolise either drug effectively
mockBank.explanation: Verapamil in WPW + AF: in AF, multiple rapid atrial impulses conduct to the ventricles. In WPW, the accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) normally has a longer refractory period than the AV node — so most impulses conduct via the AV node. Verapamil (and digoxin) blocks the AV node → all conduction diverts to the accessory pathway. If the accessory pathway has a SHORT effective refractory period (which occurs in some WPW variants), it can conduct atrial impulses at 300+ bpm directly to the ventricles → extremely rapid ventricular response → VF → cardiac arrest. This is why verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, and adenosine are ALL contraindicated in pre-excited AF/WPW. Treatment: DC cardioversion (unstable) or IV procainamide/flecainide (stable).

mockBank.question: A warfarin patient's INR is chronically difficult to control (highly labile). Which ONE of the following is the MOST important consideration before switching to a DOAC?
options: A. Labile INR is always caused by patient non-compliance and cannot be resolved by switching anticoagulants

B. Assess the indication for anticoagulation — DOACs are not approved for mechanical heart valves or rheumatic mitral stenosis; if the indication is non-valvular AF or VTE, switch to DOAC is appropriate and reduces monitoring burden
C. DOACs require more frequent monitoring than warfarin (weekly INR checks vs monthly with warfarin)
D. Labile INR is a contraindication to switching to a DOAC — DOAC levels also fluctuate widely in the same patients
E. Switch to LMWH long-term — this is always safer than both warfarin and DOACs in labile patients
mockBank.explanation: Labile INR in warfarin patients leads to increased time outside therapeutic range, greater risks of both thromboembolism (sub-therapeutic) and bleeding (supra-therapeutic). Switching to a DOAC (if the indication is appropriate) provides: predictable pharmacokinetics, no routine monitoring, and fewer drug/food interactions. CRITICAL CHECK before switching: Is the indication a mechanical heart valve or rheumatic mitral stenosis? If YES → DOACs are absolutely contraindicated (RE-ALIGN trial) — warfarin must continue despite lability. If the indication is non-valvular AF, DVT, or PE → DOACs are preferred for labile INR. The decision also considers renal function (check eGFR), bleeding risk, and patient preference.

mockBank.question: A 74-year-old man has severe heart failure with an implantable defibrillator (ICD). He attends for a full upper clearance and lower partial clearance under intravenous sedation (midazolam). Which ONE of the following considerations is MOST important during his sedation?
options: A. Midazolam is absolutely contraindicated in patients with ICDs and cannot be used under any circumstances

B. IV sedation in patients with severe heart failure requires careful monitoring: pulse oximetry, ECG monitoring, BP monitoring, and capnography; have ICD magnet and AED immediately available; avoid oversedation causing hypoxia (which could trigger ventricular arrhythmia and ICD shock)
C. The ICD must be deactivated by a cardiac physiologist before any dental sedation can proceed
D. Midazolam should be given at full standard dose as it has no cardiovascular effects
E. Regional (LA) blocks must be avoided in patients with ICDs as local anaesthetics can interfere with device sensing
mockBank.explanation: Patients with ICDs and severe heart failure who require IV sedation need careful planning: (1) The ICD does NOT need to be deactivated for dental procedures (only for situations creating EMI that could trigger inappropriate shocks — e.g. monopolar electrosurgery); (2) Monitoring requirements: continuous pulse oximetry, ECG, BP, and ideally capnography — hypoxia is the most common trigger for VT/VF and ICD shock; (3) Midazolam dosing should be reduced (hepatic congestion from heart failure slows metabolism; elderly patients are more sensitive); (4) Position: semi-reclined (not supine — heart failure + orthopnoea); (5) Have an ICD magnet available (can suspend ICD tachyarrhythmia therapy if inappropriate shocks occur during procedure); (6) AED immediately available.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following BEST describes the Killip classification and its clinical utility in MI?
options: A. Killip class I — cardiogenic shock with systolic BP <90 mmHg; Class IV — no signs of heart failure
B. Killip classification describes the degree of heart failure complicating MI: Class I (no HF signs) through Class IV (cardiogenic shock); higher class = significantly worse prognosis
C. Killip classification describes the extent of coronary artery disease on angiography
D. Killip score is used to calculate the STEMI reperfusion time window
E. Killip class relates to the number of coronary vessels diseased: I = single vessel; IV = left main coronary disease
mockBank.explanation: Killip classification (1967): describes clinical heart failure in the context of acute MI. Class I: no signs of HF (no pulmonary crackles, no S3, no raised JVP). Class II: mild HF — bilateral basal crackles (less than 50% of lung fields), S3, raised JVP. Class III: acute pulmonary oedema — crackles in >50% of lung fields, severe dyspnoea, hypoxia. Class IV: cardiogenic shock — systolic BP <90 mmHg with signs of hypoperfusion (oliguria, confusion, cold extremities). In-hospital mortality correlates with class: Class I ~5%, Class II ~10%, Class III ~40%, Class IV ~80–90%. Higher Killip class at presentation guides aggressiveness of therapy and is a key prognostic predictor in STEMI registries.

mockBank.question: A 60-year-old woman is on digoxin for AF. Her ECG shows: short PR interval, ST segment 'reverse tick' depression, and T-wave changes. She is otherwise well with a normal pulse rate. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely explanation?
options: A. These are signs of acute myocardial infarction — arrange emergency PCI
B. These ECG changes represent normal rate-controlled AF without any significance

C. These are 'digitalis effect' changes — a normal pharmacological ECG consequence of therapeutic digoxin: shortening of QT interval, scooped ST-T configuration; not a sign of toxicity if the patient is well
D. This is the ECG pattern of hyperkalaemia — check serum potassium urgently
E. The short PR interval indicates accessory pathway conduction — arrange electrophysiology study
mockBank.explanation: 'Digitalis effect' (not 'digitalis toxicity'): at therapeutic digoxin levels, the ECG may show characteristic changes from digoxin's electrophysiological effects: (1) Short QT interval (faster ventricular repolarisation); (2) Downsloping ST depression with a characteristic 'scooped' or 'reverse tick' appearance (Salvador Dali moustache pattern) — most prominent in leads with tall R-waves (lateral leads); (3) T-wave flattening or inversion. These are PHARMACOLOGICAL EFFECTS of digoxin, not evidence of toxicity. They occur at therapeutic digoxin levels and require no action. Digoxin TOXICITY signs: GI symptoms, visual disturbance (xanthopsia), bradyarrhythmias, ventricular arrhythmias, AV block — associated with elevated plasma digoxin levels and precipitating factors (hypokalaemia, renal failure).

mockBank.question: A 28-year-old woman is found to have BP 165/105 mmHg at a routine check-up. She has no symptoms and no family history of hypertension. Which ONE of the following BEST describes the appropriate initial investigation?
options: A. Start antihypertensive therapy immediately without investigation — hypertension at any age requires drug treatment

B. Screen for secondary causes of hypertension (renal disease, primary hyperaldosteronism, phaeochromocytoma, coarctation, hyperthyroidism) as the probability of secondary hypertension is higher in young patients with severe hypertension
C. Confirm on ABPM then treat with lifestyle advice only until age 40 — drug therapy is not indicated before this age
D. Refer to nephrology immediately — hypertension in a young woman always means renal disease
E. Order only renal function tests — the only relevant secondary cause in young women is renovascular disease
mockBank.explanation: Secondary hypertension is more common in: young patients (<40 years), severe hypertension, resistant to multiple drug treatments, and atypical features. Investigation for secondary causes: urinalysis and renal function (renal parenchymal disease — most common secondary cause); renal duplex ultrasound (renovascular hypertension from fibromuscular dysplasia — especially young women); aldosterone-to-renin ratio (primary hyperaldosteronism); 24-hour urinary metanephrines (phaeochromocytoma); thyroid function tests; four-limb BP ± CT angiogram (coarctation of the aorta — radio-femoral delay, rib notching). If secondary causes are excluded → essential hypertension; treat with drugs and lifestyle. The younger the patient with severe hypertension, the more important a thorough secondary cause search.

mockBank.question: A patient with rheumatic mitral stenosis in atrial fibrillation requires anticoagulation. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate anticoagulant?
options: A. Apixaban — DOACs are preferred for all forms of AF including AF with valvular disease
B. Warfarin (INR target 2–3) — the only anticoagulant proven effective and safe in AF related to rheumatic mitral stenosis; DOACs are not approved for this specific indication
C. Low molecular weight heparin lifelong — preferred as it does not require monitoring
D. Aspirin + clopidogrel — dual antiplatelet therapy is equivalent to anticoagulation in rheumatic mitral stenosis
E. Dabigatran — proven in the ENGAGE AF trial to be equivalent to warfarin for rheumatic mitral stenosis
mockBank.explanation: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are approved for 'non-valvular AF.' Rheumatic mitral stenosis represents 'valvular AF' — DOACs were NOT studied in this population in the pivotal DOAC trials and are NOT approved. The thrombus risk in rheumatic MS is higher and the mechanisms of thrombosis differ — stagnant blood in the grossly enlarged left atrium with a stenotic valve generates different fibrin composition. Warfarin (INR 2–3) remains the only evidence-based anticoagulant for AF with rheumatic mitral stenosis. Similarly, mechanical heart valves require warfarin. Bioprosthetic valves can use DOACs after the initial 3-month post-implant period. LMWH lifelong is impractical (injection-based) and lacks evidence for long-term AF anticoagulation.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the concept of 'prescribing cascade' in cardiovascular medicine and its relevance to dental care?
options: A. A prescribing cascade is when a dentist prescribes too many drugs simultaneously for a single condition
B. A prescribing cascade occurs when a drug's side effect is mistaken for a new disease, leading to another drug being prescribed to treat the 'new disease,' which may then cause further side effects
C. A prescribing cascade describes the sequential prescribing of antihypertensive drugs at step 1, 2, 3, and 4 according to NICE guidelines
D. A prescribing cascade is a patient's confusion about which drugs to take when they have multiple prescriptions
E. A prescribing cascade refers to the phenomenon where beta-blockers sequentially stop working over time, requiring escalating doses
mockBank.explanation: Prescribing cascade examples relevant to CVD/dentistry: (1) ACE inhibitor → dry cough → prescribed codeine cough suppressant → constipation → prescribed laxatives; OR (2) NSAID prescribed for dental pain → raises BP → antihypertensive dose increased → hypotension + falls; OR (3) Thiazide → hyperuricaemia → gout → NSAIDs prescribed → NSAID reduces antihypertensive effect → further antihypertensive escalation. The key principle: always consider whether a new symptom is a drug side effect before prescribing a new drug. In dental practice: before prescribing antibiotics, analgesia, or antifungals, review the patient's full drug list for potential interactions and identify if the presenting complaint may be a drug side effect rather than a new disease.

mockBank.question: A 55-year-old woman on warfarin (INR 2.6) for a prosthetic mitral valve requires replacement of a lower partial denture. During the appointment, she accidentally bites down hard on the dentist's finger and lacerates her tongue on a tooth. The tongue is bleeding freely. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate management?
options: A. Stop warfarin immediately and transfer to hospital for tongue suturing under general anaesthetic
B. Apply firm direct pressure to the tongue with a gauze pack for 20 minutes; if bleeding continues, suture the laceration under LA; do not alter warfarin; monitor for 30 minutes before discharge
C. Administer IV fresh frozen plasma to immediately reverse the warfarin effect and control tongue bleeding
D. Pack the tongue with tranexamic acid-soaked gauze as definitive treatment — no sutures needed in anticoagulated patients
E. Refer to A&E immediately as all tongue bleeds in anticoagulated patients require emergency treatment
mockBank.explanation: Tongue laceration management in a warfarin patient: (1) Firm, sustained direct pressure for 20 minutes is the first-line treatment for any laceration — this alone may achieve haemostasis in a patient with therapeutic INR (2.6 is within the 2–3 target and not excessively elevated); (2) If pressure is insufficient, suture the laceration under effective LA — simple interrupted sutures close the wound and assist haemostasis; (3) Do NOT stop warfarin — stopping risks mechanical valve thrombosis (prosthetic mitral valve has the highest thromboembolism risk); (4) FFP and hospital transfer are reserved for haemorrhage that cannot be controlled locally; (5) Tranexamic acid mouthwash is adjunctive, not definitive for an open laceration. Monitor after treatment before discharge.

mockBank.question: A 60-year-old woman with Raynaud's phenomenon and systemic sclerosis is about to undergo dental treatment under local anaesthetic in a cold dental surgery. Which ONE of the following is the MOST important precaution to take before administering the local anaesthetic?
options: A. Use prilocaine with felypressin instead of lidocaine with adrenaline as adrenaline is absolutely contraindicated in Raynaud's phenomenon
B. Warm the dental surgery to a comfortable temperature; warm the local anaesthetic solution to body temperature before injection; use minimum effective adrenaline; warm the patient's hands
C. Avoid local anaesthesia entirely and use IV sedation for all dental procedures in Raynaud's patients
D. Administer an oral dose of nifedipine before the dental appointment to pre-treat any Raynaud's episode
E. No specific modifications are needed — Raynaud's phenomenon does not affect dental anaesthesia
mockBank.explanation: Cold is the primary trigger for Raynaud's attacks (vasospasm of digital arteries). In the dental surgery: (1) Warm the room (cold environment triggers digital vasospasm even in patients who are otherwise warm); (2) Warm the local anaesthetic cartridge to body temperature (cold LA injection stings more and can trigger vasospasm response); (3) Keep the patient warm (provide blanket if needed); (4) Adrenaline in LA: systemic sclerosis/Raynaud's is a relative contraindication to excessive adrenaline (causes vasospasm of small vessels) but standard dental doses (1–2 cartridges with 1:100,000–1:200,000 adrenaline) with slow injection and aspiration are generally acceptable — the risk of inadequate anaesthesia from pain response outweighs the small adrenaline effect. Felypressin causes uterine contractions — use with caution in reproductive-age women.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly identifies the mechanism of action of levosimendan in acute heart failure?
options: A. Levosimendan inhibits phosphodiesterase (PDE3) to prevent cAMP degradation — same mechanism as milrinone

B. Levosimendan sensitises troponin C to calcium (calcium sensitiser) and opens ATP-sensitive K+ channels — improves contractility without increasing intracellular Ca2+ (less arrhythmogenic than classical inotropes) and causes vasodilation
C. Levosimendan activates beta-1 receptors in the myocardium — a sympathomimetic inotrope equivalent to dobutamine
D. Levosimendan is a vasopressin antagonist that reduces preload by promoting free water excretion
E. Levosimendan inhibits the Na/K-ATPase pump similar to digoxin, increasing intracellular calcium and contractility
mockBank.explanation: Levosimendan has a dual mechanism: (1) Calcium sensitisation — binds to troponin C in a Ca2+-dependent manner, stabilising the troponin C-Ca2+ complex and enhancing the contractile force generated per unit of intracellular calcium; importantly, this does not increase intracellular Ca2+ → less risk of arrhythmia and myocardial oxygen demand compared to conventional inotropes; (2) ATP-sensitive K+ channel opener in vascular smooth muscle → coronary and peripheral vasodilation (reduces preload and afterload). Used in acute decompensated HF when standard inotropes have failed or are poorly tolerated. Unlike dobutamine, levosimendan does not significantly increase heart rate or oxygen demand. Long half-life active metabolite provides 24-hour haemodynamic effects from a single 24-hour infusion.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following correctly describes the mechanism by which aspirin's antiplatelet effect differs from clopidogrel's, and why neither drug is a sufficient substitute for the other in all clinical scenarios?
options: A. Aspirin inhibits the P2Y12 (ADP) receptor irreversibly; clopidogrel inhibits COX-1 reversibly — they are interchangeable for most indications

B. Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1, blocking TXA2-mediated platelet aggregation; clopidogrel irreversibly blocks the P2Y12 (ADP) receptor, blocking ADP-mediated platelet aggregation. They affect different pathways — both are needed for DAPT effectiveness
C. Aspirin and clopidogrel both inhibit the GPIIb/IIIa receptor — this is why combining them has no additional benefit over using one alone
D. Aspirin inhibits factor Xa directly, while clopidogrel inhibits thrombin — they are complementary anticoagulants rather than antiplatelet agents
E. Clopidogrel is a more potent COX-1 inhibitor than aspirin, which is why it is used when aspirin resistance develops
mockBank.explanation: Platelet activation is initiated by multiple pathways. Aspirin blocks the TXA2 pathway (irreversible COX-1 acetylation → no TXA2 synthesis → less platelet activation at TXA2 receptors). Clopidogrel/ticagrelor blocks the ADP pathway (irreversible/reversible P2Y12 receptor blockade → ADP cannot amplify platelet activation). Both pathways converge on GPIIb/IIIa (final common pathway — platelet aggregation requires GPIIb/IIIa activation via both TXA2 and ADP signals). Blocking only one pathway allows the other to partially maintain platelet function. DAPT (both drugs together) achieves near-complete inhibition by blocking complementary pathways — essential for post-ACS/stent management. Replacing aspirin with clopidogrel in DAPT regimens loses the TXA2 pathway inhibition and vice versa.

mockBank.question: A 40-year-old man is prescribed sotalol for paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia. Which ONE of the following potential side effects requires monitoring on initiation?
